parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand for hotel nights in Southern Albania when the average price per night increases from ALL 4,000 to ALL 4,500.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = (360 – 400) ÷ 400 = –0.1 (–10%)  
• Percentage change in price = (4,500 – 4,000) ÷ 4,000 = 0.125 (12.5%)  
• Price elasticity of demand = (–0.1) ÷ (0.125) = –0.8

parts.content: The Albanian government estimates that an additional ALL 100 million of tourism-related spending would induce further rounds of spending through the Keynesian multiplier. If the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Albania is 0.8, calculate the total possible increase in real GDP, assuming no other changes in the economy.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.8) = 5  
• Total increase in real GDP = 5 × ALL 100 million = ALL 500 million

parts.content: Using the information in Figure 1, calculate the percentage increase in the price of Albanian footwear exports when the world price rises from ALL 3,000 per pair to ALL 3,500 per pair.
parts.markscheme:
• Change in price = 3,500 – 3,000 = ALL 500  
• Percentage increase = (500 ÷ 3,000) × 100% ≈ 16.7%

parts.content: From Figure 1, calculate the change in total export revenue for Albanian footwear if quantity exported remains at 800,000 pairs per year after the price increase from ALL 3,000 to ALL 3,500.
parts.markscheme:
• Original total export revenue = 3,000 × 800,000 = ALL 2,400,000,000  
• New total export revenue = 3,500 × 800,000 = ALL 2,800,000,000  
• Change in total export revenue = ALL 2,800,000,000 – ALL 2,400,000,000 = ALL 400,000,000

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is a tax in which a higher proportion of income is paid as income increases.

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how an increase in government spending on infrastructure (for instance, improving roads to southern coastal regions) could affect Albania’s short-run and long-run economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9621f376-0a44-4746-8b82-998a5b90ae0f)
• An initial increase in government spending shifts the AD curve rightward, raising real output and the average price level in the short run.  
• Better infrastructure also reduces costs of production over time, shifting the LRAS (and SRAS) curve to the right and contributing to potential output growth.  
• In the short run, higher output and employment may occur as aggregate demand rises.  
• In the long run, improved efficiency and productivity help sustain growth without large inflationary pressure. 

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

- 10.9% - 12.1% = -1.2%

parts.content: Using information from Table 3, explain two ways in which income inequality might pose challenges for further economic development in Albania.
parts.markscheme:
• Significant income disparity can limit overall consumption, as lower-income groups have less disposable income to spend on goods and services, which can slow growth in key sectors such as tourism and manufacturing.  
• High inequality can dampen investment in human capital; lower-income households may be unable to afford education or training, restricting labor productivity improvement.  
• This gap can perpetuate uneven development across regions, reducing social cohesion and long-term economic potential.  
• These effects can undermine the benefits of EU membership candidacy and efforts to diversify exports if parts of the population do not share in any increased prosperity.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the government of Albania could implement to reduce unemployment while managing inflationary pressures. Justify the recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Reducing unemployment while containing inflation could be addressed by supply-side policies that enhance labor productivity and lower production costs.  
• A vocational training program aimed at the large agricultural workforce could transfer surplus workers into skilled manufacturing or tourism roles.  
• Improved labor productivity would shift the long-run aggregate supply curve outward, raising potential output while dampening cost-push inflation in the long run. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/e866b46f-a24b-4f73-817f-d8bf17e93f27)
• The relatively high unemployment rate (above 10%) could be reduced as new training increases employability in the apparel and footwear industries, aligning with Albania’s goal of strengthening its manufacturing base.  
• Declining unemployment and rising output might reduce the chance of significant wage inflation if productivity advancements offset higher labor demand.  
• Funding could come from improved tax collection measures in the informal sector, helping the government balance the budget and avoid inflationary deficits.  
• A policy focus on infrastructure development, such as better transport and digital connectivity, could lower costs for businesses and attract foreign direct investment, fostering job creation and putting downward pressure on prices through greater efficiency.  
• Specific reference to the footwear and apparel sector supports export diversification, especially since higher global demand for footwear exists and improved productivity could make Albanian exports more competitive.  
• The approach of enhancing competitiveness suits a country experiencing moderate inflation while seeking sustained growth in sectors like tourism and manufacturing.  
• In conclusion, focusing on supply-side initiatives, especially comprehensive training and infrastructure improvements, is likely to reduce unemployment and contain inflation, aligning with Albania’s policy objectives of long-term economic growth and EU accession goals.

questions.specification:
CASE STUDY: Economic Developments and Challenges in Azerbaijan

1. Azerbaijan, situated at the crossroads of Eastern Europe and Western Asia, has experienced significant economic reforms over the past decade. Historically reliant on hydrocarbons-mainly oil and natural gas—for export revenues, the country has sought to diversify its production base through agriculture, tourism, and technology sectors. In 2019, oil and gas accounted for about 85% of Azerbaijan's total exports, providing substantial government revenue but leaving the country vulnerable to global commodity price fluctuations.

2. In recent years, policymakers have introduced multiple initiatives to modernize infrastructure and reduce dependence on hydrocarbons. Between 2019 and 2022, the government invested over US$3 billion in roads, railways, and energy transmission lines, aiming to expand the country's capacity to produce and transport goods. According to the Statistical Committee. Azerbaijan's real GDP grew by 2.2% in 2020 and rebounded to 4.6% in 2022, partly due to a recovery in global oil prices. However, structural unemployment persists, especially in rural areas, where older agricultural practices lag behind modern production techniques.

3. Inflation, driven by rising global commodity costs, reached 8.4% in 2022, up from 2.6% in 2019. The Central Bank of Azerbaijan responded with conservative monetary policies that have stabilized the exchange rate of the Azerbaijani manat. Meanwhile, the government introduced tariffs on certain imported agricultural goods in 2021, aiming to protect local farmers from competition and stimulate domestic production. Critics argue that these tariffs lead to higher prices for consumers, while supporters believe they create incentives for farmers to modernize and invest in capital-intensive farming techniques.

4. Foreign direct investment (FDI) centered on the energy sector remains strong, although officials are eager to attract in manufacturing and services. Improvements in transport connectivity-facilitated by ongoing infrastructure projects-have encouraged discussions about further liberalizing trade regulations to boost exports of textiles, food products, and tech services. Yet logistical barriers at border checkpoints persist, contributing to delays and raising costs for exporters looking to access regional markets.

5. Socially, Azerbaijan has implemented targeted measures to address income inequality, including subsidies for utilities and food staples. Observers note that the impact of these subsidies can be uneven; while they help low-income households cope with rising prices, they can also create fiscal pressure if oil revenues decline. The government has recently introduced pilot programs that tie subsidies to specific income thresholds, with the objective of reducing misuse of public funds.

6. Despite ambitious diversification plans, the oil sector continues to dominate. Economic analysts warn that reliance on hydrocarbons could impede sustainable growth, especially if global oil prices weaken or external demand slows. As part of its long-term development strategy, the government is promoting investment in green energy, incentivizing solar and wind power projects in the hope of creating new export opportunities for electricity.

7. Nonetheless, concerns about structural unemployment remain. Skill mismatches persist between job seekers and the needs of modern industries, particularly in the technology sector. Rural-urban migration has become more common, pressuring housing and public services in Baku, while leaving some villages with labor shortages. Recognizing these challenges, the Ministry of Education has partnered with private companies to revamp vocational education programs, aiming to align training with rapidly evolving market demands.

Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Azerbaijan (2019—2022)

| Indicator                             | 2019 | 2020 | 2021 | 2022 |      |  |
|---------------------------------------|------|------|------|------|------|--|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)            |      | 47.0 | 42.5 | 45.8 | 52.0 |  |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)              |      | 2.2  | 2.2  | 3.4  | 4.6  |  |
| Inflation Rate (%)                    | 2.6  | 3.0  | 4.5  | 8.4  |      |  |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                 |      | 5.3  | 6.5  | 6.2  | 5.9  |  |
| Exchange Rate (AZN per US$)          |      | 1.70 | 1.70 | 1.70 | 1.69 |  |
| Govt. Capital Spending (US$ billion) | 0.9  | 1.4  | 2.2  | 2.4  |      |  |

Table 2: Trade and Social Indicators

| Indicator                                         | 2019 | 2021   | 2022   |
|---------------------------------------------------|------|--------|--------|
| Oil/Gas Exports as % of Total Exports             |      | 85%    | 85%    |
| Agricultural Tariff Rate (selected products)      |      | 0%     | 10%    |
| Gini Coefficient                                  | 0.33 | 0.34   | 0.35   |
| Avg. Monthly Household Subsidy (utilities & food) |      | US$40 | US$45 |

questions.specification:
## Sustainable Growth and Economic Challenges in Vanuatu: Tourism, Trade, and Development Strategies


(1) Vanuatu is a small island nation in the southwestern Pacific Ocean, geographically dispersed across more than 80 islands. Despite its modest population of around 314,000 people, the country has seen steady growth in the tourism sector, which contributed about 22% of its Gross Domestic Product (GDP) in 2022. At the same time, agriculture remains vital, accounting for an estimated 26% of GDP, with coconut and cocoa among the leading exports.

(2) Like many small, open economies, Vanuatu faces vulnerabilities related to natural disasters and external economic shocks. In 2021, Cyclone Harold caused extensive damage to infrastructure, prompting the government to launch a major infrastructure investment program (estimated at over US$150 million) aimed at improving roads, ports, and rural connectivity. The expectation is that this will foster long-term national development by enhancing trade flows and attracting new businesses.

(3) To promote domestic production, the government introduced a direct subsidy scheme for local farmers in 2022. This program, costing approximately 2% of Vanuatu’s annual budget, aims to lower production costs and encourage food security. Officials hope that boosted agricultural outputs will reduce the country’s reliance on food imports, which remain relatively high due to limited local capacity in certain product categories.

(4) International trade plays a key role in Vanuatu’s economy. The country exports coconut oil, cocoa, and kava, primarily to regional neighbors such as Australia and New Zealand. In 2022, merchandise exports accounted for US$80 million. Meanwhile, imports of fuel, machinery, and consumer goods reached US$210 million, contributing to a persistent trade deficit. Additionally, foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows have grown, especially in areas like eco-tourism. Yet concerns persist that external shocks—including global downturns—could significantly restrict Vanuatu’s FDI prospects in the future.

(5) The national government’s economic policies include the “Sustainable Tourism Plan,” focused on eco-friendly resorts and better infrastructure to accommodate rising visitor numbers. The authorities envision that a more environmentally conscious approach to tourism development could both generate revenue and protect Vanuatu’s natural habitats.

(6) Meanwhile, inflation stood at around 3.2% in 2021, inching up to 3.5% in 2022, partly due to rising fuel costs. Budget deficits have been moderate, averaging around 3% of GDP over the last three years. The government has been managing external debt cautiously, working with international organizations on concessional lending terms to finance climate-resilient projects and the new infrastructure plan, while striving to maintain fiscal sustainability.

(7) Alongside its focus on macroeconomic stability, Vanuatu is confronted with social issues such as uneven access to education and healthcare, especially in remote islands. Recent data shows that 15% of rural households have limited access to healthcare. The authorities are hopeful that targeted spending on public services, underwritten by the new infrastructure projects, will improve living standards.

(8) Looking forward, Vanuatu’s policymakers see opportunities to diversify the economy by building more resilient supply chains and expanding niche exports, such as organic produce. However, achieving strong economic development depends largely on climate adaptation measures, the effective use of infrastructure investment, and managing the balance between tourism growth and environmental sustainability.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Vanuatu (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                       | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|---------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ million)       | 880    | 900    | 920    | 950    |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 3.1    | 0.8    | 1.2    | 2.5    |
| Inflation Rate (%)              | 2.7    | 2.9    | 3.2    | 3.5    |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)       | -2.5   | -3.0   | -3.2   | -3.1   |

**Table 2: Social and Trade Indicators (2020–2022)**

| Indicator                               | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|-----------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|
| Population (thousands)                 | 308   | 311   | 314   |
| Tourism Receipts (US$ million)         | 160   | 180   | 220   |
| Total Merchandise Exports (US$ million)| 70    | 75    | 80    |
| Total Merchandise Imports (US$ million)| 205   | 210   | 210   |
| FDI Inflows (US$ million)              | 22    | 25    | 30    |

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, discuss the likely impact of Vanuatu’s Sustainable Tourism Plan on the country’s economic growth and development.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definition**  
- “Sustainable tourism” is tourism designed to minimize environmental impact while contributing to local economic development.  
- “Economic growth” is an increase in the real output of an economy over time, measured by real GDP.  
• “Economic development” refers to improvements in living standards, reduction in poverty, and greater access to healthcare and education.

**Likely Impact on Economic Growth**
- The Plan’s focus on eco-friendly resorts may boost tourism receipts 
.- This can raise aggregate demand, stimulating real GDP growth.  
- The tourism sector’s heightened revenues can generate higher employment, income, and consumption, further boosting GDP.  
- Higher government spending, consumer expenditures and tourism (counts as exports) all contribute to higher Aggregate Demand and shift the AD1 rightward to AD2. 

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/05cd98c3-6310-43da-acbb-dd2627228c20)

- Infrastructure investment (US$150 million) related to tourism (e.g. roads, ports) reduces transport costs and supports trade, raising productivity in the long run.  
- However, reliance on external factors such as global economic conditions could limit tourism growth if international demand falls, restricting the plan’s impact on GDP.

**Likely Impact on Economic Development**
- Increased tax revenues from tourism can be used for public spending on healthcare and education, especially in rural areas where 15% of households have inadequate healthcare (paragraph 7).  
- Sustainable tourism initiatives protect natural habitats, potentially preserving resources for future generations and attracting higher-value eco-conscious tourists.  
- The plan might diversify the economy if visitors also purchase local organic products (coconut, cocoa, kava), supporting agriculture and encouraging local entrepreneurship.  
- Environmental concerns: larger tourist numbers may strain ecosystems, especially if regulations are weak. This could reduce long-term sustainability and harm local communities if overuse or pollution occur.

Balanced Discussion (Opportunities vs. Challenges)  
- On one hand, Vanuatu could enjoy higher GDP growth, increased employment, and revenue for public services. The country’s natural beauty can be preserved through eco-friendly policies.  
- On the other hand, the expansion of tourism could still pose environmental risks; if ecosystems are damaged, future tourists may decline. Infrastructure investments may also incur opportunity costs, diverting funds from alternative development projects.  
- Vulnerability to external shocks remains a concern: a global downturn or natural disaster can undermine tourism flows.  
- Overall, if well-managed, the Sustainable Tourism Plan can strike a balance between stimulating economic growth and ensuring equitable development without compromising the environment.

**Conclusion**
- The Sustainable Tourism Plan has the potential to increase real GDP and improve living standards, especially if accompanied by effective regulation and investment in social services.  
- Success depends on the government’s ability to manage environmental externalities, maintain prudent fiscal and monetary policies, and handle external shocks.  
- In the long run, a responsible tourism strategy can contribute to more diversified growth and a higher level of sustainable development in Vanuatu.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that economic growth worsens income equality.
parts.markscheme:
**Definition:**

• Economic growth: An increase in real GDP over time, indicating that an economy’s output of goods and services is increasing.  
• Income inequality: The uneven distribution of income within a population, often measured using tools such as the Gini coefficient or represented using a Lorenz curve.  

**Economic Theory:**

• Modern growth theory suggests that sustained increases in a country’s real output can expand overall national income, which can potentially reduce absolute poverty but does not necessarily translate into more equal income distribution.  

 • In the early stages, capital owners and skilled labor tend to benefit disproportionately from industrialization, widening the income gap.  
 -  Over time, as education spreads to a broader segment of society and economic opportunities diversify, income inequality can stabilize or decline through wider mobility.  
• Market-based economies can experience “trickle-down” effects if increased profits for firms lead to productivity improvements, higher wages for workers, and more jobs; however, these effects can be uneven, particularly if high-skilled labor receives a disproportionately large share of the gains.  

• The Lorenz Curve illustrates income distribution by plotting the share of income against the share of the population. The further it deviates from the line of equality, the greater the income inequality, making it a useful tool to analyze how economic growth may impact equity, especially when considering the role of government intervention.  The Gini coefficient, derived from the Lorenz Curve, quantifies inequality on a scale from 0 (perfect equality) to 1 (maximum inequality).

•Government intervention, through taxation, minimum wages, or social welfare programs, can influence income distribution by redistributing resources and setting wage floors. These policies can shift the Lorenz Curve closer to the line of equality, indicating reduced inequality, or, if poorly designed, may have the opposite effect.
- Progressive taxation, redistributes income by taxing higher earners at higher rates to reduce inequality. Such policies can help offset the unequal effects of economic growth by supporting lower-income groups.

**Diagram:**

• A Lorenz curve can illustrate how rising aggregate income can be unequally distributed.  
• Shifts in the Lorenz curve closer or further from the line of equality highlight whether growth is inclusive or skewed towards certain groups.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/eabd153a-ae8e-4c8d-8730-0df4613dc912)

**Evaluation:**

• Short-Run and Long-Run Impact on Stakeholders  
  – In the short run, rapid economic growth can disproportionately concentrate wealth concentrate wealth in the hands of capital owners and highly skilled individuals,  widening income inequality, potentially widening inequality for low-skilled workers and increasing the gap between rural and urban regions.  
  – In the long run, if government policy and market forces support widespread education, job creation, and upward mobility, growth can reduce inequality by broadening access to higher-paying opportunities. However, if wealth accumulation becomes established and limits access to resources without redistributive measures, long-term inequalities may worsen for marginalized groups.

**Real-World Data and Examples:**

  – During China’s early growth phases (1980s–1990s), rapid industrial expansion lifted hundreds of millions out of absolute poverty but also caused a surge in urban-rural income disparities. The Gini coefficient rose from around 0.30 in the early 1980s to above 0.46 by the early 2000s, indicating that growth initially worsened income inequality.  
  – In Brazil, sustained growth in the early 2000s combined with targeted social policies (such as Bolsa Família) helped reduce the Gini coefficient from approximately 0.59 in the 1990s to around 0.53 by 2010. Although inequality remained high, income growth, alongside social transfers, moderated the extent to which economic expansion skewed income distribution.  
  – In the United States, since the 1980s, strong technological advances and economic expansion benefitted high-skilled workers, with real wages for top earners growing much faster than for low-skilled labor. The Gini coefficient approached 0.49 by 2019, reflecting persistent inequality despite high overall GDP growth.

**Conclusion:**

  – Economic growth can initially worsen income inequality, such as in China's case, especially when capital and high-skilled labor capture most benefits. Over the long term, if accompanied by effective redistribution and broad access to education, growth need not perpetuate widening income gaps.
 
- Real-world evidence reveals that policy interventions can shape whether growth narrows or widens income inequality (such as Brazil) by altering access and redistribution, making the outcome highly context-dependent.

questions.specification:
Dominica is a small island economy in the Caribbean with a population of approximately 72 000 people. The country is heavily reliant on agriculture and tourism. Hurricanes and tropical storms frequently disrupt economic activity, causing significant damage to infrastructure, agriculture, and tourism facilities. Despite these challenges, Dominica’s government has introduced various strategies to improve economic growth and reduce poverty.

In recent years, Dominica has sought to diversify away from agriculture (particularly bananas), moving toward eco-tourism and financial services. However, agriculture still plays a vital role in the economy, especially in rural areas where employment opportunities outside farming are limited.

Table 1 below shows selected macroeconomic indicators for Dominica for 2021 and 2022:

| Indicator                        | 2021   | 2022   |
|----------------------------------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (EC$ millions)*      | 1 720  | 1 817  |
| Real GDP growth (%)              | 3.0    | 5.2    |
| Unemployment rate (%)            | 11.9   | 10.5   |
| Inflation rate (%)               | 1.5    | 3.8    |
| Government spending (EC$ millions) | 470    | 490    |
| Average monthly banana exports (tonnes) | 1 800  | 1 950  |

*EC$ = Eastern Caribbean dollars

Table 2 shows data on Dominica’s banana exports to a regional trading partner and estimated price elasticity of demand (PED) for bananas in that market from 2021 to 2022:

| Year | Quantity of bananas exported (tonnes) | Price per tonne (EC$) | Estimated PED |
|------|---------------------------------------|-----------------------|---------------|
| 2021 | 1 800                                 | 800                   | –0.6          |
| 2022 | 1 950                                 | 840                   | –0.6          |

Dominica’s tax system consists of a value-added tax (VAT), corporate income tax, and personal income tax. Some taxes are progressive, helping to reduce income inequality. Table 3 displays key features of Dominica’s tax rates (approximate) and Gini coefficient values before and after implementation of new social programs in 2022:

| Measure                                       | Rate / Value                  |
|-----------------------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| Standard VAT rate                              | 15 %                          |
| Reduced VAT rate (applied to essential goods)  | 10 %                          |
| Corporate income tax                           | 25 %                          |
| Personal income tax (top marginal rate)        | 35 %                          |
| Gini coefficient (2021)                        | 0.42                          |
| Gini coefficient (2022, after social programs) | 0.40                          |

Figure 1 (hypothetical) shows a simplified Keynesian multiplier effect in Dominica following an increase in government spending in 2022:

• Initial injection into the economy: EC$20 million  
• Estimated marginal propensity to consume (MPC): 0.8  

Government officials are debating policies to promote economic diversification, attract foreign direct investment, and reduce inequality.

parts.content: Using the information provided in Table 1, calculate the approximate percentage increase in Dominica’s nominal GDP from 2021 to 2022.
parts.markscheme:
• Subtract 2021 GDP (EC$1720 million) from 2022 GDP (EC$1817 million) to find the increase: 1817 – 1720 = 97.  
• Divide 97 by 1720 and multiply by 100: (97 ÷ 1720) × 100 ≈ 5.64%.  
• Final answer: approximately 5.6%.

parts.content: Using the data provided on banana exports in Table 2 and assuming the PED remains constant at –0.6, calculate the percentage change in quantity demanded if the price per tonne were to increase by 5 % from its 2022 level.
parts.markscheme:
• The formula for percentage change in quantity demanded: (%ΔQ) = PED × (%ΔP).  
• PED = –0.6, %ΔP = +5%.  
• (%ΔQ) = –0.6 × 5% = –3%.  
• Final answer: quantity demanded would decrease by approximately 3%.

parts.content: Using Figure 1’s information on the Keynesian multiplier effect (initial injection of EC$20 million with an MPC of 0.8), calculate the total change in real GDP in Dominica resulting from this injection.
parts.markscheme:
• The multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC) = 1 ÷ (1 – 0.8) = 5.  
• Total change in real GDP = 5 × EC$20 million = EC$100 million.

parts.content: Assume that the government decides to increase government spending further by EC$15 million. Using the same MPC of 0.8, calculate the additional change in real GDP in Dominica generated by this new spending.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier = 5.  
• Additional change in real GDP = 5 × EC$15 million = EC$75 million.

parts.content: Define the term “progressive tax.”
parts.markscheme: • A progressive tax is one in which the tax rate increases as an individual’s income rises, meaning those with higher incomes pay a proportionally higher rate of tax.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the percentage change in total revenue from banana exports between 2021 and 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Revenue in 2021: 1,800 × 800 = EC$1,440,000
- Revenue in 2022: 1,950 × 840 = EC$1,638,000
- Change in revenue: 1,638,000 - 1,440,000 = EC$198,000
- Percentage change: (198,000 ÷ 1,440,000) × 100 = 13.75%
- Percentage change in total revenue: 13.8% (or 13.75%)

parts.content: Using information from the text above, explain two ways in which dependence on agriculture might leave Dominica vulnerable to external shocks.
parts.markscheme:
• Frequent hurricanes damage crops and infrastructure, reducing agricultural output and export revenues, which undermines farmers’ earnings and the country’s GDP.  
• Fluctuations in global or regional demand and prices for important crops like bananas can sharply affect rural incomes, leading to instability in employment and foreign exchange earnings.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the government of Dominica could implement to reduce income inequality while maintaining economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
• Income inequality is the unequal distribution of income among individuals or households within an economy, while economic growth is an increase in real output over time.  
• A policy that targets enhanced skills training and education programs in rural and urban areas can reduce inequality by improving employability and productivity; this can expand the long-run aggregate supply while increasing the earning potential of lower-income groups.  
• Dominica’s reliance on agriculture and the data showing a reduction in the Gini coefficient from 0.42 to 0.40 suggest that social programs have already helped, so additional targeted spending in vocational education or infrastructure upgrades could amplify this effect.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/1ca4d93a-6f31-4fe2-ae69-ff53772f7fb5)
• Strengthening infrastructure (roads, communication networks) in rural regions supports agricultural efficiency and fosters eco-tourism growth, expanding economic activity and job opportunities.  
• Such government spending can be financed through slightly higher progressive taxation or reallocation of expenditure, ensuring that high-income earners contribute more while avoiding excessive distortions to investment.  
• The multiplier effect in Dominica is relatively large (as indicated by the information on MPC = 0.8); continued injections into skills development and infrastructure can stimulate aggregate demand and support higher output.  
• Greater human capital formation improves the productivity of the non-farm sector, diversifies the economy beyond agriculture, and reduces vulnerability to storms and price shocks.  
• The approach balances equity (by improving wages for the lower-income population and limiting regressive effects) and efficiency (by growing productive capacity through better-trained labor).  
• For long-term impact, policies that encourage foreign direct investment in sectors like eco-tourism and financial services widen employment opportunities, solidify growth prospects, and reduce inequality if linked to skill-development initiatives.  
• Sustained reduction in the Gini coefficient and stable real GDP growth are likely outcomes if the government invests in human capital, expands progressive measures, and promotes economic diversification.

questions.specification:
Poland is a country in Central Europe with a population of approximately 38 million. Its membership in the European Union (EU) has contributed to strong economic ties with other European nations. In recent years, Poland has experienced both robust economic growth and rising inflationary pressures. While unemployment remains low, concerns about income inequality have drawn the government’s attention.

Poland is also one of the largest producers of apples in the EU, exporting a significant portion of its harvest to other European countries, especially Germany. However, rising production costs and shifting demand conditions have affected both domestic producers and international consumers of Polish apples. Discussions surrounding potential government interventions include tax policy changes, labor market incentives, and expanded social welfare programs. The statistics below provide insights into macroeconomic performance, apple market dynamics, taxation, and household consumption behavior in Poland.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Data for Poland** 

| Indicator                                     | 2021        | 2022        |
|----------------------------------------------|-------------|-------------|
| Real GDP (billion PLN, at 2015 prices)       | 2200        | 2310        |
| Inflation rate (CPI, %)                      | 5.1         | 14.3        |
| Unemployment rate (%)                        | 3.4         | 3.0         |
| Gini coefficient                             | 0.30        | 0.32        |
| Population (millions)                        | 38.1        | 38.2        |
| Estimated average Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) | 0.8         | 0.8         |

**Table 2: Apple Market Data (Exports to Germany)**

| Price per kilogram (euros) | Quantity Demanded (thousand metric tons) | Quantity Supplied (thousand metric tons) |
|----------------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------------------|
| 2.00 (in 2021)             | 600                                      | 620                                      |
| 2.40 (in 2022)             | 540                                      | 700                                      |

**Table 3: Corporate Taxation in Poland (2022)**

| Type of tax                       | Rate                         |
|----------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Corporate Income Tax (CIT)       | 19%                          |
| Personal Income Tax (progressive)| 17% up to certain threshold, 32% beyond |
| Furniture manufacturer’s net profit (million PLN) | 15                         |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the approximate real GDP growth rate for Poland between 2021 and 2022.
parts.markscheme:
- Subtract 2200 from 2310 = 110  
  - Divide 110 by 2200 and multiply by 100% ≈ 5%

parts.content: The Polish government decides to increase its expenditure by 5 billion PLN. Assuming the Marginal Propensity to Consume (MPC) in Poland remains at 0.8, calculate the total potential change in real GDP that may result from this increase in government spending.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiplier = 1 ÷ (1 − 0.8) = 5  
  - Total change in real GDP = 5 billion PLN × 5 = 25 billion PLN

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Polish apples in Germany when the price increases from €2.00 to €2.40 per kilogram.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity demanded = (540 − 600) ÷ [(600 + 540) ÷ 2] × 100%  
  - Percentage change in price = (2.40 − 2.00) ÷ [(2.00 + 2.40) ÷ 2] × 100%  
  – PED ≈ −0.57

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for Polish apples over the same price range.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity supplied = (700 − 620) ÷ [(700 + 620) ÷ 2] × 100%  
  - Percentage change in price = (2.40 − 2.00) ÷ [(2.00 + 2.40) ÷ 2] × 100%  
  - PES ≈ 0.67

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend one policy that the government of Poland could implement to address the challenges posed by both high inflation and rising income inequality. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Define key terms: inflation (a sustained increase in the general price level) and income inequality (the unequal distribution of income).  
- Explain the theoretical link: elevated inflation erodes purchasing power, disproportionately affecting lower-income groups; simultaneously, income inequality impairs consumption, reduces social mobility, and could diminish long-term growth.  
- Refer to the case study: Poland’s inflation rate surged to 14.3%, and the Gini coefficient increased to 0.32, reflecting growing inequality. The need is to alleviate the burden on lower-income households while stabilizing prices. 
- Propose policy within the syllabus: recommend a targeted fiscal policy through means-tested subsidies or direct income support to low-income households. A possible action involves reducing VAT on essential goods to mitigate price pressures, thereby addressing inflation’s immediate impact.  
- Cutting VAT on essentials lowers costs, shifting SRAS right from SRAS1 to SRAS2. This lowers the price level (pl1 -> pl2), easing cost-push inflation and increases output (Y1 -> Y2). AD stays the same, so the new equilibrium means lower inflation and higher growth, matching the policy’s aim.


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/39ba1cda-faee-4b30-b884-eb6c2b3d7417)

- Highlight how the recommended policy addresses inflation: lowering indirect taxes on basic goods can reduce cost-push elements and moderate price increases; the support for low-income groups helps maintain consumption without adding excessive inflationary pressure.  
- Connect to long-term growth: narrowing income inequality may enhance human capital investment, increasing productivity. In Poland’s case, this fosters stable AD growth, reducing potential social tensions and strengthening economic performance.  
- Evaluate potential drawbacks: reduced tax revenue can strain the government budget, leading to higher public debt if not managed. However, short-term revenue loss may be offset by improved productivity and healthier consumer demand in the long run.  
- Conclude the recommendation: targeted fiscal measures that reduce living costs for lower-income households dampen the adverse effects of inflation, while also narrowing the income gap to support more sustainable economic growth.

questions.specification:
Greenland is an autonomous territory within the Kingdom of Denmark, with a population of approximately 56,000. Its economy depends heavily on fishing, particularly for shrimp and halibut, which account for about 90% of its total export revenues. In recent years, Greenland has also experienced steady growth in the tourism sector, with more visitors attracted by the region’s unique Arctic landscapes. 

Due to harsh weather conditions and limited arable land, Greenland’s domestic production of agricultural goods is minimal, and most food is imported. Real GDP has shown modest growth in the last several years, supported by fishing exports and increasing public expenditure aimed at improving infrastructure. However, concerns have been raised about inequalities in income distribution, high dependence on a single export (fish), and the sustainability of public finances.

Below are selected data and tables on Greenland’s economy.

**Table 1: Key macroeconomic indicators for Greenland (2018–2021**)

| Year | Real GDP (million DKK) | Real GDP growth (%) | Population | Unemployment Rate (%) |
| ---  | -----------------------| --------------------|------------|----------------------- |
| 2018 | 18,700                 | 2.0                | 56,000     | 5.8                   |
| 2019 | 19,100                 | 2.1                | 56,200     | 5.5                   |
| 2020 | 19,200                 | 0.5                | 56,300     | 6.2                   |
| 2021 | 19,600                 | 2.1                | 56,400     | 5.9                   |

**Table 2: Market data for fish in Greenland (annual domestic demand)**

(Prices in DKK per kilogram)

| Price per kg | Quantity Demanded (tons) |
| ------------ | ------------------------ |
| 40           | 12,000                  |
| 45           | 11,600                  |
| 50           | 11,100                  |
| 55           | 10,300                  |

**Table 3: Distribution of income in Greenland and selected countries**

| Country    | Gini Coefficient | % of Population below national poverty line |
|------------|------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| Greenland  | 0.33            | 9%                                          |
| Denmark    | 0.28            | 5%                                          |
| Iceland    | 0.25            | 6%                                          |

**Table 4: Tax rates in Greenland (2021)**

| Type of tax          | Rate of tax              |
|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Corporate income tax | 25%                     |
| Personal income tax  | Progressive up to 42%*  |
| Indirect tax (VAT)   | 15%                     |

*Higher marginal tax rates may apply above certain income thresholds.

Government spending on infrastructure has been steadily increasing in Greenland, especially in the construction and improvement of new ports to accommodate more cruise ships and cargo vessels. It is estimated that every 100 million DKK spent on infrastructure increases real GDP by 120 million DKK, suggesting a potential Keynesian multiplier effect of 1.2 in the local economy.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1, calculate Greenland’s average annual rate of real GDP growth for the entire period 2018–2021.
parts.markscheme:
- Add the annual growth rates: 2.0% + 2.1% + 0.5% + 2.1% = 6.7%.  
- Divide by 4 to find the average: 6.7% ÷ 4 = 1.675%.  
- The average annual rate of real GDP growth is approximately 1.7%.

parts.content: The government of Greenland plans to invest an additional 200 million DKK in port infrastructure. Assuming the Keynesian multiplier for Greenland is 1.2, calculate the total increase in real GDP resulting from this additional investment.
parts.markscheme:
- Multiply the injection by the multiplier: 200 million DKK × 1.2 = 240 million DKK.  
- The total increase in real GDP is 240 million DKK.

parts.content: Using the information in Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for fish in Greenland when the price increases from 45 DKK/kg to 50 DKK/kg.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate percentage change in quantity demanded: ((11,100 − 11,600) ÷ 11,600) × 100% ≈ −4.31%.  
- Calculate percentage change in price: ((50 − 45) ÷ 45) × 100% ≈ 11.11%.  
- PED ≈ −4.31% ÷ 11.11% ≈ −0.39 (absolute value ≈ 0.39).

- The change in total spending is 33,000 DKK.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend one policy which the government of Greenland could implement to reduce reliance on fishing exports. In the answer, refer to how this policy might affect macroeconomic goals such as growth, employment, and income distribution.
parts.markscheme:
- Diversification of the economy can be pursued by incentivizing the development of alternative industries, such as expanding tourism or promoting small-scale manufacturing.  
- Economic diversification reduces dependence on fishing exports by creating new sources of revenue, aligning with the existing growth in tourism and taking advantage of Greenland’s Arctic landscapes.  
- Government support can include targeted subsidies or tax incentives for private firms to invest in tourism infrastructure beyond port facilities, stimulating the emergence of related service industries and attracting foreign direct investment.  
- Increased output in the tourism or alternative sectors contributes to higher real GDP over time, especially if the multiplier effect spreads through local communities.  
- Employment might rise in hospitality, transport services, and marketing fields, potentially reducing unemployment from current levels shown in Table 1.  
- Income distribution may improve if new job opportunities in alternative sectors draw workers from various income brackets, reducing the concentration of income in fishing alone.  
- Income inequality could be addressed by ensuring training programs, so the labor force is equipped to work in new industries, which helps lower-income groups.  
- Government subsidies and tax incentives boost investment and consumption, causing AD to shift right (AD1 -> AD2). This leads to a higher real GDP (Y1 -> Y2) and higher price level (pl1 -> pl2), showing short-run economic growth. The diagram captures how demand-side policies can stimulate output without necessarily causing inflation if there's spare capacity.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ced8f236-b30e-40b3-9894-cb27c98718e0)

- Careful monitoring of government finances is necessary to manage the cost of subsidies or infrastructure projects, minimizing long-term debt risks.  
- Pursuing alternative industries would mitigate external vulnerabilities (such as fluctuations in global fish prices) and underpin long-term sustainable development of Greenland’s economy.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that there is a trade-off between low unemployment and low inflation.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions
1. **Unemployment**: When people of working age (16-65) who are actively seeking and able to work, but are not employed.
2. **Inflation**: A sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over a period of time.
3. **Phillips Curve**: An economic concept that illustrates an inverse relationship between the rate of unemployment and the rate of inflation in an economy.

### Economic Theory
- **Phillips Curve Explanation**:
  - The Phillips Curve suggests a short-run trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
  - When unemployment is low, demand for labor increases, leading to higher wages.
  - Higher wages increase consumer spending, boosting demand for goods and services, which can lead to higher prices (inflation).
  - Conversely, high unemployment leads to lower demand for labor, reducing wage pressures and potentially lowering inflation.
- **Monetarist Perspective**:
  - In the long run, the Phillips Curve is vertical, indicating no trade-off between inflation and unemployment.
  - Inflation is primarily a monetary phenomenon, determined by the growth rate of the money supply.
  - Attempts to reduce unemployment below its natural rate can lead to accelerating inflation without reducing unemployment in the long run.
- **Diagram**:
  - **Short-Run Phillips Curve (SRPC)**: Downward sloping, showing the inverse relationship between inflation and unemployment.
  - **Long-Run Phillips Curve (LRPC)**: Vertical at the natural rate of unemployment, indicating no long-term trade-off.

### Diagram
- **Short-Run and Long-Run Phillips Curve**:
  - **SRPC**: Illustrates the trade-off between inflation and unemployment in the short run.
  - **LRPC**: Shows that in the long run, the economy returns to the natural rate of unemployment regardless of inflation.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/edd849c8-5354-42cc-b591-af487dcbc127)
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/11b16238-1dce-4e2e-8110-7fcfbee57db6)


### Evaluation
- **Real-World Example**: United States in the late 1960s and 1970s.
  - During the 1960s, the U.S. experienced low unemployment and rising inflation, consistent with the Phillips Curve.
  - In the 1970s, stagflation (high inflation and high unemployment) challenged the traditional Phillips Curve, leading to the development of the expectations-augmented Phillips Curve.
- **Stakeholders**:
  - **Workers**: Benefit from low unemployment due to higher job security and wages but may suffer from reduced purchasing power if inflation rises.
  - **Businesses**: May face higher costs due to wage increases, potentially reducing profit margins.
  - **Government**: Balances policies to manage inflation and unemployment, considering political and economic implications.
- **Long-Run vs. Short-Run**:
  - In the short run, policies targeting low unemployment may lead to higher inflation.
  - In the long run, the economy may adjust, and the trade-off may not hold, as seen in the vertical LRPC.
- **Advantages vs. Disadvantages**:
  - **Advantages**: Short-term economic growth and reduced unemployment.
  - **Disadvantages**: Potential for accelerating inflation and reduced purchasing power.
- **Prioritize**:
  - Policymakers must prioritize based on current economic conditions, such as whether the economy is in a recession or experiencing inflationary pressures.

### Conclusion
1. The trade-off between low unemployment and low inflation is primarily a short-run phenomenon, as illustrated by the Phillips Curve.
2. In the long run, the trade-off may not exist, as suggested by monetarist theories and the vertical LRPC.
3. Real-world examples, such as the U.S. experience in the 1970s, highlight the complexity and limitations of the Phillips Curve in explaining the relationship between unemployment and inflation.

parts.content: Explain how a minimum wage causes structural unemployment in an economy.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions
1. **Minimum Wage**: The lowest legal remuneration that employers can pay their workers.
2. **Structural Unemployment**: Structural unemployment happens when there is a mismatch between workers’ skills and available jobs.
3. **Labor Market**: The supply and demand for labor, where employees provide the supply and employers the demand.

### Diagram
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a590f609-c402-4c0f-a0d2-b45338d719dd)
- **Diagram**: Labor Market Diagram
  - **Axes**: Wage rate (vertical axis) and quantity of labor (horizontal axis).
  - **Curves**: Labor supply curve (upward sloping) and labor demand curve (downward sloping).
  - **Indication**: Show the equilibrium wage and quantity of labor. Indicate the minimum wage above the equilibrium wage, leading to a surplus of labor (unemployment).

### Explanation
- **Introduction**: 
  - Define minimum wage and structural unemployment.
  - State that a minimum wage set above the equilibrium wage can lead to structural unemployment.

- **Step-by-Step Inductive Reasoning**:
  - **Market Equilibrium**:
    - In a free labor market, the equilibrium wage is determined by the intersection of labor supply and demand.
    - At this point, the quantity of labor supplied equals the quantity of labor demanded.

  - **Imposition of Minimum Wage**:
    - Introduce a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage.
    - This creates a price floor in the labor market.

  - **Impact on Labor Demand and Supply**:
    - At the higher minimum wage, the quantity of labor demanded by employers decreases because labor becomes more expensive.
    - Simultaneously, the quantity of labor supplied increases as more workers are willing to work at the higher wage.

  - **Creation of Unemployment**:
    - The gap between the quantity of labor supplied and the quantity of labor demanded at the minimum wage level represents unemployment.
    - This unemployment is structural because it results from the wage being artificially set above the market-clearing level, causing a mismatch.

  - **Mismatch of Skills**:
    - Over time, the skills of unemployed workers may not match the skills required by employers, exacerbating structural unemployment.
    - Workers may need retraining or relocation to find employment, which is not immediately available.

- **Additional Explanation**:
  - **Long-term Effects**:
    - Persistent structural unemployment can lead to a loss of skills and human capital, making it harder for workers to find jobs even if the minimum wage is adjusted.
    - It may also lead to increased government spending on unemployment benefits and retraining programs.

- **Conclusion**:
  - Summarize how a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage can lead to structural unemployment by creating a surplus of labor and a mismatch of skills in the economy.

parts.content: Explain the different causes of unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
**Answers may include:**

### Definitions
1. **Unemployment**: When people of working age (16-65) who are actively seeking and able to work, but are not employed.
2. **Cyclical Unemployment**: Cyclical unemployment occurs during economic downturns when demand for goods and services falls, leading to job losses.
3. **Structural Unemployment**: Structural unemployment happens when there is a mismatch between workers’ skills and available jobs.


### Explanation
- **Cyclical Unemployment**:
  - Occurs during periods of economic recession or downturn.
  - Illustrated by a leftward shift in the AD curve in the AD-AS model.
  - As AD decreases, firms produce less, leading to a reduction in the demand for labor.
  - This results in higher unemployment as firms lay off workers.
  - Example: During a recession, consumer confidence falls, reducing consumption and investment, which decreases AD.

- **Structural Unemployment**:
  - Arises from changes in the economy that alter the demand for certain skills.
  - Can be illustrated using a labor market diagram where the supply of labor exceeds demand at the current wage rate.
  - Often caused by technological advancements or shifts in industries (e.g., decline of manufacturing jobs).
  - Workers need retraining or relocation to find new employment opportunities.
  - Example: Automation in manufacturing leads to a decline in demand for manual labor, increasing unemployment among workers with outdated skills.

- **Frictional Unemployment**:
  - Occurs when workers are temporarily unemployed while transitioning between jobs.
  - Considered a natural form of unemployment as it reflects the time taken for job search and matching.
  - Not typically illustrated with a diagram but is important to mention as part of the natural rate of unemployment.
  - Example: A recent graduate looking for their first job or a worker who voluntarily leaves a job to find a better one.

  - **Seasonal Unemployment**: Occurs when demand for labor fluctuates with the season (e.g., tourism, agriculture).
  - **Classical Unemployment**: Results from wages being above the equilibrium level, often due to minimum wage laws or union activities.

  - **Natural Rate of Unemployment**: The sum of frictional and structural unemployment, representing the unemployment rate when the economy is at full employment.
  - Policies to reduce unemployment may include demand-side policies (e.g., fiscal stimulus) for cyclical unemployment and supply-side policies (e.g., retraining programs) for structural unemployment.

### Diagram
- **AD-AS Model**: The diagram should illustrate a leftward shift in Aggregate Demand (AD) leading to cyclical unemployment. It should show the initial equilibrium and the new equilibrium with lower output and higher unemployment.
- **Labor Market Diagram**: This can be used to show structural unemployment, where the supply of labor exceeds demand at the prevailing wage rate.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/f8a38631-9180-45ca-a086-6533db98ec88)


![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/fd9f8580-57f9-4dc9-a984-269f3287da8c)

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the view that the most significant consequence poverty is a decrease in economic growth potential.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions
1. **Poverty**: The inability to meet standard consumption needs. These standards can be absolute or relative.
2. **Economic Growth**: An increase in a country's real output (real GDP) over a period time.
3. **Economic Growth Potential**: The maximum possible output an economy can achieve when all resources are fully and efficiently utilized.

### Economic Theory
- **Link between Poverty and Economic Growth Potential**:
  - **Human Capital**: Poverty limits access to education and healthcare, reducing human capital development. Lower human capital diminishes labor productivity, which is crucial for economic growth.
  - **Investment**: High poverty levels can lead to lower savings and investment rates, as individuals prioritize immediate consumption over savings. This reduces capital accumulation, a key driver of economic growth.
  - **Market Size**: Poverty reduces the purchasing power of a significant portion of the population, limiting domestic market size and discouraging investment in production capacity.
  - **Social Stability**: High poverty can lead to social unrest, which can deter investment and disrupt economic activities, further hindering growth potential.

### Diagram
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/56e88b8b-8dd9-4fac-a782-a5c04ab3190a)
- **Production Possibility Curve (PPC)**:
- Include an inwards shift from PPC1 to PPC2, showing fewer quantities of Good X and Good Y produced.

### Evaluation
- **Stakeholders**: 
  - **Government**: Faces increased pressure to provide social welfare, which can strain public finances and reduce funds available for growth-enhancing investments.
  - **Businesses**: May experience reduced demand for goods and services, limiting their growth and profitability.
  - **Individuals**: Those in poverty face limited opportunities for upward mobility, perpetuating the cycle of poverty and limiting overall economic growth.

- **Long-run vs. Short-run**:
  - **Short-run**: Immediate effects of poverty may include reduced consumer spending and lower aggregate demand, leading to slower economic growth.
  - **Long-run**: Persistent poverty can lead to structural issues such as a poorly educated workforce and inadequate infrastructure, significantly reducing growth potential.

- **Advantages vs. Disadvantages**:
  - **Advantages**: Addressing poverty can lead to a more equitable distribution of resources, potentially increasing social cohesion and stability, which are conducive to growth.
  - **Disadvantages**: High levels of poverty can lead to increased crime and health issues, which can further strain economic resources and reduce growth potential.

- **Prioritize**:
  - While poverty is a significant factor affecting economic growth potential, other factors such as technological innovation, political stability, and global economic conditions also play crucial roles. Therefore, while addressing poverty is essential, it should be part of a broader strategy to enhance growth potential.

- **Real-world Example**: 
  - **India**: Despite rapid economic growth, India faces significant poverty levels. According to the World Bank, over 20% of the population lives below the national poverty line. This has implications for India's growth potential, as seen in challenges related to education and healthcare access. Efforts to reduce poverty, such as the Mahatma Gandhi National Rural Employment Guarantee Act (MGNREGA), aim to improve income levels and, consequently, economic growth potential.

### Conclusion
1. Poverty significantly impacts economic growth potential by limiting human capital development and investment.
2. Addressing poverty can enhance growth potential, but it should be part of a comprehensive strategy that includes other growth-enhancing measures.
3. Real-world examples, such as India, illustrate the complex relationship between poverty and economic growth potential, highlighting the need for targeted policy interventions.

questions.specification:
The United Kingdom (UK) is one of the largest economies in Europe, with its gross domestic product (GDP) predominantly driven by services, manufacturing, and financial activities. In 2021, the UK economy showed signs of recovery from the global downturn of 2020, despite ongoing uncertainties related to international trade and supply chain disruptions. The government remains concerned about persistent income inequality, slow productivity growth, and a continuing current account deficit.

Due to strong demand and limited housing supply, average house prices in the UK have risen sharply in recent years, prompting concerns of an affordability crisis. Economists estimate that the price elasticity of supply (PES) for new homes is relatively low (0.60 in the short run), while the demand for houses tends to be price inelastic. The UK government has also introduced various fiscal policies to stimulate growth. In 2022, an infrastructure spending package of £20 billion was announced, with some analysts estimating a marginal propensity to consume (MPC) of 0.8, implying a potentially significant multiplier effect on aggregate demand.

Below are selected data on macroeconomic indicators, the housing market, and the UK taxation system.

Table 1: UK Macroeconomic Indicators (2020–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (in £ billions) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) | Government Spending (in £ billions) | Net Exports (in £ billions) | Gini Coefficient |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2020 | 2000  | -9.9 | 350  | -20 | 0.34 |
| 2021 | 2160  | 8.0  | 370  | -30 | 0.35 |
| 2022 | 2270  | 5.1  | 390  | -25 | 0.35 |

Table 2: UK Housing Market Data (2021)

| Quarter | Average House Price (£) | Number of Houses Sold (000s) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Q1  | 270 000  | 290 |
| Q2  | 280 000  | 295 |
| Q3  | 310 000  | 285 |
| Q4  | 320 000  | 280 |

Table 3: Summary of UK Personal Income Tax Rates (2022)

| Income bracket (£ per year) | Income tax rate (%) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 – 12 570       | 0  |
| 12 570 – 50 270  | 20 |
| 50 271 – 150 000 | 40 |
| Over 150 000     | 45 |

Additionally, value-added tax (VAT) in the UK is charged at 20% on the majority of goods and services. In June 2022, a construction firm purchased new machinery for £120 000 (plus VAT) to increase its capacity for building new homes. 

Despite substantial exports of financial services and manufactured goods, the UK continues to run a negative net export figure. Some policymakers worry that an ongoing current account deficit could hinder long-term economic performance, while many economists are also focused on reducing the Gini coefficient of 0.35, considered moderate but persistent in the UK context.

parts.content: Explain why a persistent current account deficit might be considered a macroeconomic concern for the UK.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/0e0262eb-e88e-414d-9c22-6d201146cc4b)
A persistent current account deficit indicates that imports consistently exceed exports, leading to potential borrowing from abroad. It exerts downward pressure on the domestic currency while increasing foreign debt obligations. There is a risk of eroding confidence among international investors and creating vulnerabilities should foreign financing slow or become more expensive. It may also reduce long-term growth if debt servicing diverts resources from domestic investment.

parts.content: Using the information in the text, explain how the Keynesian multiplier could impact the level of real GDP following the £20 billion government spending initiative.
parts.markscheme: The Keynesian multiplier suggests that an initial government spending injection leads to multiple rounds of new spending. With an MPC of 0.8, the multiplier amplifies the initial £20 billion, resulting in a larger increase in aggregate demand than the initial injection. This additional spending circulates through the economy, raising consumption, output, and employment. The overall effect on real GDP is greater than the original spending due to repeated expenditure cycles.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the UK government could implement to address the persistent current account deficit.
parts.markscheme:
• A policy that reduces the persistent current account deficit could involve an expenditure-switching approach, for example, a targeted export subsidy designed to make domestic exports more price-competitive in global markets.  
• A current account deficit occurs when the value of imports exceeds the value of exports for an extended period, which is reflected in the negative net export values in Table 1.  
• Export subsidies could lower producers’ costs, encouraging more production for overseas markets, thus boosting export revenue.  
• Greater export competitiveness could address the UK’s negative net export figure (−20 in 2020, −30 in 2021, −25 in 2022), helping to close the deficit by switching demand away from imports.  
• This policy could also mitigate some of the concerns that a persistent deficit raises, such as mounting foreign debt or downward pressure on the currency.  
• Expanding exports has the potential to strengthen aggregate demand, improving real GDP and reducing unemployment, supported by the UK’s strong services and manufacturing sectors.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8674cad6-80a6-48e1-b87f-dc782c9374e9)
• The multiplier effect may enhance the benefits of growing exports if additional export revenue circulates domestically, stimulating consumption and investment.  
• However, there is a risk of retaliation from trading partners if subsidies are perceived as anti-competitive, which could lead to trade disputes and reduce overall gains in the long run.  
• The government could moderate this risk by ensuring that subsidies comply with international trade rules and by combining them with broader supply-side policies that improve productivity and long-term competitiveness.  
• Implementing this policy addresses the case study’s concern over the current account deficit by reinforcing the UK’s export base and moving the external balance closer to equilibrium while also enhancing economic performance.

parts.content: Explain how the imposition of a minimum wage can lead to structural unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition:

- **Minimum Wage**: A legally set wage floor below which employers cannot pay their workers, typically established by the government.

- **Structural Unemployment**: A form of unemployment arising from a mismatch between the skills offered by workers and the skills demanded by employers, often due to changes in the structure of the economy or labor market.

### Economic Theory:

- A minimum wage set above the equilibrium wage (price We at quantity Qd/s e) (where labor demand equals labor supply) may result in a labor surplus because firms reduce their demand of labor (Qd) while more individuals are willing to work at the higher wages (Qs).

- This resulting surplus of labor from the wage floor causes unemployment to arise as shown by the difference between Qs and Qd on a labor market diagram with the new minimum wage.

- Over time, certain groups of workers, especially those with lower skill levels, may be at a higher risk of remaining unemployed if firms choose to hire workers with more specialized skills who can justify the higher wage.

- Further, the unemployed segment of the labor force may find their skills becoming obsolete if firms shift to more capital-intensive production methods in response to higher wage costs. This technological substitution and changing labor demand can create a structural mismatch where available jobs require different or higher skill levels than unemployed workers possess.

- Prolonged unemployment for these workers can worsen skill obsolescence and reduce employability, increasing structural unemployment in the economy. 

- In conclusion, setting minimum wages can contribute to structural unemployment if firms in response decide to use labor-saving technologies and shift production processes,  making certain workers' skills less relevant at the higher wage rate.

### Diagram:

- A labor market diagram illustrating a minimum wage above the equilibrium wage.

- The labor supply (Qs) is greather than the labor demand (Qd) at the minimum wage level, and the difference between demand and supply represents the surplus of labor (unemployment).

questions.specification:
Greece is a country in southeastern Europe that has faced significant economic challenges over the past decade. Despite recent signs of recovery, with the economy expanding in 2022 after a prolonged recession, Greece continues to experience relatively high unemployment and concerns about income inequality. Tourism remains a major contributor to GDP, accounting for around 25% of economic activity, while shipping generates about 21% of the country’s export revenue. Another prominent export is olive oil, widely recognized for its quality in the global market.

The Greek government imposes a progressive income tax system, with tax rates rising at higher income brackets. While attempts have been made to strengthen tax collection processes, tax evasion and inefficiencies still pose challenges. Moreover, consumption is subject to a 24% value-added tax (VAT) on most goods and services, although some essential items enjoy reduced rates.

In 2022, Greece’s real GDP growth rate reached 5.9%, supported by a strong rebound in tourism and improving global economic conditions. Nonetheless, the unemployment rate remains elevated at 12.9%. Income distribution, measured by a Gini coefficient of approximately 0.33, suggests moderate inequality, prompting policymakers to consider fiscal and structural measures to reduce disparities. The data below provide insights into the country’s economic performance and market structures.

Table 1: Greek Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2022)

| Year | Real GDP (billion €) | Nominal GDP (billion €) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Real GDP Growth Rate (%) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 2018 | 173 | 181 | 19.3 | 1.9 |
| 2019 | 176 | 185 | 17.3 | 1.7 |
| 2020 | 165 | 168 | 16.4 | -6.3 |
| 2021 | 172 | 180 | 14.7 | 4.2 |
| 2022 | 182 | 193 | 12.9 | 5.9 |

Table 2: Market Data for Greek Olive Oil Exports to the EU

| Price per litre (€) | Annual Quantity Demanded (million litres) | Annual Quantity Supplied (million litres) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 4.00 | 110 | 95 |
| 4.50 | 105 | 100 |
| 5.50 | 95 | 105 |

Table 3: Selected Income Tax Brackets in Greece

| Annual Income Bracket (€) | Marginal Tax Rate (%) |
| --- | --- |
| 0 – 10,000 | 9 |
| 10,001 – 20,000 | 22 |
| 20,001 – 30,000 | 28 |
| 30,001 – 40,000 | 36 |
| Above 40,000 | 44 |

Table 4: Income Distribution in Greece (selected data)

| Income Group | Percentage Share of Total Income (%) |
| --- | --- |
| Top 10% | 23 |
| Next 30% | 34 |
| Bottom 60% | 43 |
| Gini Coefficient | 0.33 |

Percentage change in price: [(5.50 - 4.50) ÷ 4.50] × 100 = 22.22%
PES = 5 ÷ 22.22 = 0.225 (or 0.23)

parts.content: With reference to Figure 1, explain how the Keynesian multiplier might affect Greece’s real GDP following an increase in government expenditure on infrastructure.
parts.markscheme:
• An initial injection of government spending on infrastructure generates additional rounds of spending by firms and households, multiplying the initial effect on aggregate demand.  
• Figure 1 shows a multiplier between 1.2 and 1.4, indicating that each euro spent leads to a greater overall increase in national income.  
• As people earn more from the spending, they consume more, creating further demand for goods and services.  
• This cycle results in a final increase in real GDP greater than the original government injection.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Greek government could implement to reduce the relatively high unemployment rate. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Unemployment can be defined as the portion of the labor force that is actively seeking work but unable to find a job.  
• A suitable policy to reduce unemployment is an expansionary fiscal policy that focuses on infrastructure projects and targeted subsidies for industries such as tourism and shipping.  
• Higher government spending on infrastructure can shift aggregate demand to the right, as indicated by the data showing strong multiplier effects (Figure 1), enabling a larger final increase in real GDP.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/095ef7bd-b9b9-4c78-8476-444c948f2d7d)
• Funding improvements in tourism facilities and port infrastructure builds on the 25% GDP share from tourism and 21% export revenue from shipping, creating new jobs in construction, services, and complementary sectors.  
• Improved infrastructure is likely to attract more visitors, raising demand for labor in tourism-related businesses, helping to reduce the 12.9% unemployment rate reported in 2022.  
• The policy targets Greece’s high unemployment by directly creating construction and service jobs, leading to more consumption and investment, supported by the moderate Gini coefficient (0.33), which indicates room for spending to lower inequalities.  
• There is a risk of higher budget deficits and potential inefficiencies, but stronger tax collection and a progressive taxation system help finance these projects, countering the concern about growing public debt.  
• Some structural reforms in labor markets could accompany fiscal expansion, reducing labor inefficiencies and tax evasion issues noted in the text, maximizing the employment impact.  
• The anticipated result is a virtuous cycle of job creation, higher household incomes, and additional tax revenue that can fund further development, addressing concerns of persistent joblessness.  
• Overall, an expansionary fiscal policy directed toward improving tourism and shipping infrastructure, coupled with efficiency improvements in tax collection, reduces unemployment while leveraging Greece’s core economic sectors.
