parts.content: Explain why Malaysia’s reliance on palm oil exports might pose a risk to its long-term external stability.
parts.markscheme:
• Dependence on a single primary commodity can lead to vulnerability if global palm oil prices fall, reducing export revenue.  
• Price volatility in commodity markets can lead to fluctuating foreign exchange earnings, making external accounts unstable.  
• Shifts in consumer preferences or environmental regulations can quickly reduce export demand, harming the balance of payments.  
• Over-reliance on palm oil limits economic diversification, leaving the economy exposed to external shocks.

parts.content: Using information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of supply for palm oil in Malaysia when the price increases from US$620 to US$750 per metric tonne. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Percentage change in quantity supplied: [(1.95 - 1.80) ÷ 1.80] × 100 = 8.33%
- Percentage change in price: [(750 - 620) ÷ 620] × 100 = 20.97%
- PES = 8.33 ÷ 20.97 = 0.40
- Price elasticity of supply: 0.40

parts.content: Using information from the text, explain how a falling unemployment rate might affect Malaysia’s long-term economic development.
parts.markscheme:
• Lower unemployment indicates more people in work, raising incomes and improving living standards.  
• A growing workforce can increase aggregate output, promoting higher long-term economic growth.  
• Greater employment opportunities may reduce income inequality if wage earnings rise, aligning with government goals.  
• Higher productivity and investment can result from an expanding labor force, supporting long-term development.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the Malaysian government to reduce income inequality.
parts.markscheme:
• “Income inequality” and “progressive taxation” represent key terms. Income inequality refers to the uneven distribution of income across the population. A progressive tax system is one in which the tax rate increases as income rises.  
• Higher progressive taxation on upper brackets can generate more government revenue, which can be redistributed through public spending and welfare programs. This can address persistent inequality suggested by the Gini coefficient (0.41 in 2021 to 0.40 in 2022).  
• Increasing tax rates specifically in higher income brackets or closing loopholes ensures that those with greater ability to pay contribute more, while minimizing the burden on lower incomes. Reference to Table 3 shows that the current highest rate is 28%. Adjusting this rate could intensify redistributive effects.  
• Government expenditure funded by additional tax revenue could target essential services such as education, healthcare, and skills training, promoting equal opportunities and reducing the reliance on palm oil for employment. This supports the diversification strategy highlighted in the case study.  
• Allocating part of the increased revenue to direct transfer payments for lower-income individuals could reduce immediate financial difficulties and increase aggregate demand, consistent with the multiplier effect described (MPC of 0.75).  
• In the long run, educating and training the workforce can sustain productivity growth, expanding potential output and reducing the risk of over-reliance on commodities. This aligns with the government’s plan for sustainable development and structural reforms.  
• This approach would tackle income inequality by directly redistributing income, helping lower-income groups while maintaining economic stability. An increase in their disposable income also supports domestic industries and encourages growth, further consolidating the long-term external stability sought by policymakers.  
• The policy integrates well with current fiscal measures and works alongside programs that promote job placement, potentially lowering unemployment and supporting broader-based growth.  
• Reducing inequality in this manner could help maintain social cohesion, ensuring that the benefits of economic expansion are shared more evenly.  
• The recommended policy uses progressive taxation as a direct tool to achieve equity, which is consistent with Malaysia’s emphasis on reducing the Gini coefficient and developing a more resilient economy.

questions.specification:
## UZBEKISTAN’S ONGOING ECONOMIC TRANSFORMATION

Over the last decade, Uzbekistan, a doubly landlocked country in Central Asia with a population of approximately 34 million in 2021, has embarked on a series of ambitious reforms aimed at liberalizing its economy. Real GDP growth averaged around 5.8% per year between 2018 and 2021, supported by rising exports of cotton, gold, and natural gas. However, dependence on primary commodities poses risks to external balance, and the government has signaled intentions to diversify into textile manufacturing, tourism, and information technology.

State-led modernization has included partial price liberalization in agriculture, attracting foreign direct investment (FDI). A notable policy change involved easing bureaucratic barriers for foreign investors, enabling the launch of new small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). While privatization of state assets in the textile and apparel industries has progressed, concerns remain regarding efficiency gains for local businesses. Anecdotal evidence shows that some domestically owned farms still struggle to access affordable credit, hampering investments in irrigation systems and modern equipment.

The government’s push toward a more open economy coincided with substantial exchange rate reforms. In 2017, the som was devalued, and the central bank began allowing more market-driven fluctuations. This policy aimed to improve exporters’ competitiveness and encourage remittance inflows. By 2021, the exchange rate stood at around UZS 10,600 per US dollar. Although this move pleased some international organizations, it also contributed to temporary spikes in imported inflation, which reached 12.4% in 2021.

Meanwhile, Uzbekistan contends with persistent poverty, especially in rural regions. While official estimates showed a poverty rate of nearly 15% in 2021, independent analyses suggest it could be higher when accounting for underemployment in the informal sector. The government has introduced targeted cash transfers and expanded vocational training to help break the so-called “poverty cycle,” in which low income, limited savings, and lack of education reinforce each other. Development agencies recommend further investments in human capital and infrastructure to tackle income inequality—whose Gini coefficient has hovered around 0.36 in recent years.

On the trade front, Uzbekistan has moved to reduce average tariff rates on specific imports, particularly machinery crucial for agribusiness modernization. In addition, the country is in deliberations to join several regional trade blocs to increase export opportunities. Critics argue that remaining administrative barriers continue to complicate cross-border exchanges and inflate the costs of imports, which could stifle productivity growth in domestic industries that rely on foreign technology.

Monetary policy remains aimed at managing high inflation. Although the central bank has softened some liquidity constraints to facilitate credit expansion, the policy rate has been kept relatively high to stem inflationary pressures. The government hopes that stable macroeconomic conditions, alongside structural reforms, will draw in greater inflows of both portfolio investments and FDI to diversify the economic base beyond raw materials.

Infrastructure improvements are also underway, including the construction of new road and railway links to neighboring countries. Advocates argue these projects enhance regional integration, while skeptics caution that excessive reliance on foreign loans could worsen external debt burdens. Moreover, concerns about corruption and inefficient public spending persist, calling for enhanced transparency measures.

Human development indicators reflect a mixed picture. Literacy rates exceed 99%, but the national unemployment rate for youth reached 9.1% in 2021. The government has responded by emphasizing job creation through SME support programs and rural development projects. Uzbekistan’s World Bank “Ease of Doing Business” ranking has improved significantly since 2016, yet some local entrepreneurs still cite unpredictable regulations and limited access to finance as ongoing constraints.

Although reforms are moving Uzbekistan toward a more market-oriented economy, policymakers face complex trade-offs among controlling inflation, safeguarding impoverished communities, and ensuring stable economic growth. The trajectory of these reforms—and especially their impact on poverty—will depend on consistent policy implementation, regional integration initiatives, and continued progress in governance and institutional capacity.

Below are two tables that provide more detailed information on Uzbekistan’s recent economic indicators and development progress:

**Table 1: Key Macroeconomic Indicators (2018–2021)**

| Indicator                       | 2018    | 2019    | 2020    | 2021    |
|---------------------------------|---------|---------|---------|---------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)       | 50.3    | 57.2    | 59.7    | 62.5    |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 5.1     | 5.6     | 3.0     | 7.4     |
| Inflation Rate (%)              | 14.3    | 13.0    | 11.8    | 12.4    |
| Budget Deficit (% of GDP)       | -1.8    | -2.6    | -4.3    | -3.5    |
| Exchange Rate (UZS per US$)     | 8100    | 9400    | 9900    | 10600   |
| Unemployment Rate (%)           | 7.9     | 7.2     | 8.7     | 9.1     |

**Table 2: Selected Development Indicators (2021)**

| Indicator                                 | Value |
|-------------------------------------------|-------|
| Poverty Rate (% of population)           | 15.0  |
| Gini Coefficient                          | 0.36  |
| FDI Inflows (US$ billion)                | 1.3   |
| Literacy Rate (%)                         | 99.3  |
| Average Tariff Rate (%)                  | 9     |
| Share of Agriculture in GDP (%)          | 28    |
| Remittances (% of GDP)                   | 12    |

parts.content: (i) Define the term “bureaucratic barriers” (paragraph 2).
parts.markscheme:
Bureaucratic barriers  
A form of administrative or regulatory obstacles (such as licenses, permits, lengthy procedures) imposed by government agencies that increase costs and complexity, potentially slowing investment or business activity.

parts.content: (ii) Define the term “tariff” (paragraph 7).
parts.markscheme:
Tariff  
A tax imposed on imported goods and services, usually designed to protect domestic industries and/or raise government revenue by increasing import prices relative to domestic products.

parts.content: (i) Using information from Table 1, calculate the absolute increase in Uzbekistan’s nominal GDP (in US$ billions) from 2018 to 2021.
parts.markscheme:
Calculation of the absolute increase in nominal GDP  
• From Table 1, nominal GDP in 2018 was US$50.3 billion, and in 2021 it was US$62.5 billion.  
• Therefore, the absolute increase = 62.5 – 50.3 = US$12.2 billion.

parts.content: (ii) Sketch an AD/AS diagram to show how rising inflation (Table 1) could be influenced by changes in aggregate demand.
parts.markscheme:
AD/AS diagram to show rising inflation from increased aggregate demand  
• When aggregate demand (AD) increases (e.g., due to higher investment and exports), it shifts outward from AD1 to AD2.  
• An outward shift of AD in the short run, with aggregate supply (AS) relatively unchanged, leads to a higher general price level (inflation).  
• Graphically, the intersection of AD2 and AS is at a higher price level than the intersection of AD1 and AS.

parts.content: Using a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, explain how reducing bureaucratic barriers to FDI might affect the supply of foreign currency in Uzbekistan’s foreign exchange market.
parts.markscheme:
Reducing bureaucratic barriers to FDI and the supply of foreign currency in Uzbekistan’s foreign exchange market  
Reducing bureaucratic barriers makes it easier for foreign investors to set up operations in Uzbekistan. This likely increases FDI inflows, as more foreign currency enters the country (because foreign investors must convert foreign currency into som when investing). On a demand-and-supply-of-currency diagram, the supply curve of foreign currency shifts to the right, lowering the exchange rate (in terms of UZS per US$) or at least exerting downward pressure on it, assuming other factors remain constant.

parts.content: Using a monetary policy diagram (showing money supply and interest rates), explain how the central bank’s relatively high policy rate could affect inflation and economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
The central bank’s relatively high policy rate and its effects on inflation and growth  
Keeping a high policy rate discourages excessive borrowing and helps reduce inflationary pressure by dampening aggregate demand. However, it can also slow economic growth by discouraging investment and consumption financed by credit. In a monetary policy diagram (with interest rate on the vertical axis and quantity of money on the horizontal axis), a relatively high interest rate corresponds to a stricter monetary policy stance, controlling inflation but potentially reducing real GDP growth.

questions.specification:
Ghana is one of the leading cocoa producers in the world, second only to Côte d’Ivoire. Cocoa has long served as a major source of export revenue, accounting for up to 20% of total merchandise exports. In addition to cocoa, Ghana depends on mineral exports, especially gold. The services sector has expanded in recent years, contributing significantly to Ghana’s GDP.

Between 2021 and 2022, Ghana’s real GDP grew by an estimated 4.2%. However, rising global commodity prices have increased Ghana’s inflation rate to over 30% in 2023, a situation complicated by higher costs of imported fuel and raw materials. The country has also faced a widening fiscal deficit due to increased government expenditure and relatively low tax revenue collection.

In an effort to diversify Ghana’s exports, the government has introduced incentives for local entrepreneurs, including lower corporate income taxes for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Still, many economists argue that structural challenges, such as income inequality and limited infrastructure, could hinder long-term economic development. The Gini coefficient for Ghana stands at approximately 0.43, reflecting moderate income inequality. 

The Ghanaian government has been exploring both demand-side and supply-side policies to spur more robust and inclusive growth. On the demand side, it has increased spending on infrastructure (roads and energy), while on the supply side, it has encouraged private investment in processing facilities for cocoa and other agricultural products, hoping to move up the value chain.

TABLE 1: DOMESTIC DEMAND AND SUPPLY DATA FOR COCOA IN GHANA

| Cocoa Price (US$/tonne) | Quantity Demanded (thousand tonnes) | Quantity Supplied (thousand tonnes) |
|-------------------------|--------------------------------------|--------------------------------------|
| 2200                    | 710                                  | 530                                  |
| 2500                    | 680                                  | 560                                  |

Notes:  
• In 2022, Ghana exported 560 thousand tonnes of cocoa out of its total production.  
• World market prices for cocoa increased from US$2200 per tonne to US$2500 per tonne over the last harvest season.  

TABLE 2: SELECTED MACROECONOMIC INDICATORS FOR GHANA

| Indicator                              | 2021    | 2022    | 2023 (est.) |
|----------------------------------------|---------|---------|-------------|
| Real GDP (US$ billions)               | 67      | 70      | 73          |
| Annual Real GDP Growth Rate (%)        | 3.5     | 4.2     | 4.0         |
| Tax Revenue (GH₵ billions)            | 55.0    | 58.8    | 64.0        |
| Government Expenditure (GH₵ billions) | 70.0    | 76.2    | 85.0        |
| Inflation Rate (%)                     | 12.5    | 25.0    | 31.5        |
| Gini Coefficient                       | 0.43    | 0.43    | 0.43        |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                  | 11.2    | 11.5    | 12.0        |

Notes:  
• GH₵ refers to Ghanaian cedi (national currency).  
• The government has implemented a progressive personal income tax system with rates ranging from 0% up to 30%.  
• The corporate income tax rate in Ghana is 25%.  
• The value-added tax (VAT) rate on consumer goods is 15%.

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for cocoa in Ghana when the price increases from US$2200 per tonne to US$2500 per tonne.
parts.markscheme:
• Percentage change in price = [(2500 – 2200) ÷ 2200] × 100 ≈ 13.64%  
• Percentage change in quantity demanded = [(680 – 710) ÷ 710] × 100 ≈ –4.23%  
• PED = (–4.23%) ÷ (13.64%) ≈ –0.31

parts.content: Ghana’s marginal propensity to consume (MPC) is estimated at 0.75. Using this information, calculate the Keynesian multiplier, showing all your steps.
parts.markscheme:
• Multiplier formula = 1 ÷ (1 – MPC)  
• 1 – 0.75 = 0.25  
• 1 ÷ 0.25 = 4

parts.content: Using an AD/AS diagram, explain how a sharp increase in the price of imported fuel could create cost-push inflation in Ghana’s economy.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/8a423e83-1f3d-4a88-a8a5-549284fd1b9c)

• Higher fuel prices raise production costs for firms, shifting the short-run aggregate supply (SRAS) curve leftward.  
• This leftward shift causes the average price level to rise, leading to an increase in inflationary pressures.  
• Real output tends to contract temporarily as firms face higher costs of production.  
• The immediate effect is known as cost-push inflation because the trigger is an increase in costs rather than an increase in aggregate demand. 

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the price elasticity of supply (PES) for cocoa in Ghana when the price increases from US$2200 per tonne to US$2500 per tonne. Show your working.

parts.content: Using information from the text and data above, explain two ways in which a high Gini coefficient might act as a barrier to economic development in Ghana.
parts.markscheme:
• Unequal income distribution can limit the purchasing power of lower-income groups, reducing overall demand for local goods and services and hindering growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which could be implemented by the government of Ghana in order to address the country’s rising inflation rate. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
• Inflation is the sustained increase in the general price level of goods and services in an economy over time.  
• Monetary policy involves the central bank adjusting interest rates and the money supply to influence overall economic activity, including the rate of inflation.  
• Ghana’s inflation has climbed to over 30%, partly due to higher global commodity prices and imported fuel costs, suggesting both cost-push and demand-pull elements.  
• Tightening monetary policy by raising interest rates could help reduce aggregate demand and stabilize prices by discouraging excessive consumer and business borrowing. 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ca3cdbb3-98a9-4be0-b8b4-b7619ec3336b)
• Higher interest rates may slow down real GDP growth initially, but they can help anchor inflation expectations and currency stability, especially given rising import costs.  
• Contractionary monetary policy could reduce imported inflation by making the Ghanaian cedi relatively stronger if higher interest rates attract foreign capital.  
• Table 2 shows notable increases in government expenditure, which can add demand-pull pressures; complementary fiscal restraint (reducing some government spending) could reinforce the effect of tighter monetary policy.  
• A coordinated approach that combines moderate interest rate hikes with targeted government spending cuts in non-priority projects could address Ghana’s fiscal deficit while stabilizing the price level.  
• Structural issues such as income inequality (Gini coefficient of 0.43) and limited infrastructure remain challenges to sustainable growth, but containing inflation is crucial for long-term investment and confidence.  
• Implementing a carefully paced contractionary monetary policy, complemented by prudent fiscal measures, is recommended to lower inflation, maintain investor confidence, and support a more stable macroeconomic environment over time.

parts.content: Explain how aggregate demand is likely to be affected by an increase in consumer confidence.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition 
- **Consumer confidence**: A measure of how optimistic consumers are about future income and the overall economic outlook, which influences the level of spending and saving.  
- **Aggregate demand (AD)**: The total demand for all goods and services in an economy at a given price level over a given time period, calculated as \( \text{AD} = C + I + G + (X - M) \).  
- **Consumption (C)**: Household expenditure on goods and services; it typically makes up the largest portion of aggregate demand.  


### Explanation / Economic Theory  
- Consumer confidence is a critical determinant of consumption (C), which is one of the main components of aggregate demand (AD).  
- When consumer confidence rises, households expect higher or more stable future incomes and improved employment prospects.  
- This heightened optimism leads to a lower propensity to save and a higher propensity to consume, increasing the consumption component of aggregate demand.  
- As consumption increases, the aggregate demand curve shifts to the right (from AD1 to AD2) in the AD/AS framework.  
- A rightward shift of the AD curve typically raises both real output (Y) and the average price level (PL) in the short run.  
- Higher spending by consumers can trigger a **multiplier effect**, where one person’s spending becomes another person’s income, further reinforcing the rise in aggregate demand.  
- The overall impact is higher equilibrium real output and a possible increase in the general price level in the short run, assuming other components of AD and supply-side factors remain constant.  


### Diagram  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/9cf603ab-590f-471e-b48f-29ee4a70af20)
- A standard **AD/AS diagram** can be used to illustrate the effect:  
  - **Horizontal axis**: Real output (Y)  
  - **Vertical axis**: Average price level (PL)  
  - **AD curve** shifts rightward (AD1 to AD2) to reflect the increase in consumption due to higher consumer confidence.  
  - **Short-run aggregate supply (SRAS)** remains the same unless changes in costs of production or other supply-side factors occur.  
  - **New equilibrium**: Higher real output and potentially higher price level, showing how increased consumption drives aggregate demand upward.  

### Conclusion  
- An increase in consumer confidence raises household spending (consumption), shifting aggregate demand to the right.  
- This leads to higher levels of real output and may cause an increase in the price level in the short run, reflecting the typical impact of stronger consumer confidence on the macroeconomy.

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate the effectiveness of fiscal policy in reducing a nation’s rate of unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definition  
- **Fiscal policy**: Government decisions on taxation and spending intended to influence aggregate demand (AD) and overall economic activity.  
- **Unemployment**: The situation in which individuals who are actively seeking employment are unable to find a job, often measured as a percentage of the labor force. 
- **Rate of unemployment**: The percentage of the total labor force that is unemployed but actively seeking employment.



### Explanation / Economic Theory  
- Governments implement expansionary fiscal policy (higher government spending and/or lower taxes) to shift aggregate demand (AD) to the right.  
   - When AD increases, firms respond by hiring more workers to meet higher demand, reducing cyclical unemployment.  
   - This shift can be demonstrated with an AD–AS (Aggregate Demand–Aggregate Supply) diagram:  
     - The initial equilibrium is at the intersection of AD1 and AS, where real output is Y1 and the average price level is PL1.  
     - Expansionary fiscal policy shifts AD1 to AD2.  
     - The new equilibrium shows a higher real output (Y2) and a potentially higher average price level (PL2).  
     - As output increases, firms demand more labor, lowering unemployment.  
- In the short run, expansionary fiscal policy stimulates economic growth by increasing consumption (through lower taxes) and investment (through improved business confidence if government spending is targeted effectively).  
- In the long run, fiscal policy can also address structural unemployment if it allocates spending to education, training, and infrastructure projects that enhance labor productivity.  
- However, expansionary fiscal policy could lead to a budget deficit if government spending surpasses tax revenue.  
   - If the deficit persists, governments may need to borrow, possibly increasing national debt.  
   - High government debt can lead to higher interest rates (crowding out) and future tax burdens.  



### Diagram  
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/848ab236-3f7c-4f42-80c5-fdd44c5c76cd)
- A standard AD–AS diagram can illustrate how an increase in government spending (or a cut in taxes) shifts the AD curve rightward.  
- Vertical axis: Average price level (PL).  
- Horizontal axis: Real output (Y).  
- Initial equilibrium: Intersection of AD1 and AS (real output at Y1, price level at PL1).  
- After expansionary fiscal policy: AD shifts to AD2, equilibrium moves to (Y2, PL2).  
- Result: Higher real output and lower unemployment in the short run.  


### Evaluation  

- **Short-run and long-run impacts on major stakeholders**  
  - In the short run, expansionary fiscal policy can boost growth and employment, beneficial for consumers through increased job opportunities and higher disposable incomes.  
  - In the long run, higher debt repayments may limit the government’s future capacity to spend, possibly affecting producers if taxes rise to service debt.  

- **Real-world examples**  
  - After the 2008 global financial crisis, the United States government introduced the American Recovery and Reinvestment Act (2009) worth approximately USD 787 billion.  
    - Unemployment fell from around 10% in 2009 to roughly 8.3% by 2012, indicating a moderate success in reducing joblessness.  
    - Nonetheless, critics pointed to increased federal debt, which exceeded 100% of GDP in subsequent years.  
  - In response to the COVID-19 pandemic, several European nations, including Germany, launched significant fiscal stimulus packages in 2020 and 2021.  
    - Germany’s stimulus measures exceeded EUR 130 billion, contributing to a faster recovery in employment levels by late 2021.  
    - The European Commission noted that public debt in the Eurozone rose above 95% of GDP, creating concerns about long-term fiscal sustainability.  
  - Expansionary fiscal policy often depends on existing economic conditions:  
    - If the economy is near full employment, increased government spending may create inflationary pressure rather than new jobs.  
    - If unemployment is largely structural, fiscal stimulus focusing on job training programs could prove more effective than broad spending alone.  


### Conclusion: 
- Fiscal policy can be effective in reducing unemployment, especially during periods of significant economic slack, but potential crowding out and long-term debt burdens must be balanced.
- Use of supply side policies with fiscal policies can reduce a nation's rate of unemployment more effectively as it will address all the types of unemployment (cyclical, structural, frictional, seasonal).

questions.specification:
Indonesia is the world’s fourth most populous country, with over 270 million inhabitants. It is a major emerging economy in Southeast Asia, well-known for its natural resources, robust agricultural sector, growing manufacturing base, and diverse export portfolio. Palm oil, coal, coffee, and rubber are among the major export commodities. Despite strong economic growth, Indonesia still faces challenges related to improving infrastructure, reducing reliance on primary sector exports, and tackling income inequality.

The reliance on palm oil exports has raised concerns regarding both environmental sustainability and the vulnerability of export earnings to fluctuations in international prices. Additionally, ongoing population growth places more pressure on government spending and infrastructure development. Meanwhile, Indonesia’s progressive tax system and other policies aim to raise revenue for social spending; however, issues in tax compliance and collection persist. Policymakers are also exploring new ways to boost aggregate demand and promote equitable development.

Below are several tables and a figure that provide more data about Indonesia’s economy.

**Table 1: Domestic Demand for Palm Oil (2021)**

| Price per tonne (US$) | Quantity Demanded (million tonnes/year) |
|-----------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| 700                   | 22                                      |
| 750                   | 19                                      |
| 800                   | 17                                      |
| 850                   | 15                                      |

**Table 2: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators**

| Year | Real GDP (US$ billions) | Population (millions) | GNI per capita (US$) |
|------|-------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| 2017 | 1,016                   | 262                    | 3,850               |
| 2018 | 1,042                   | 265                    | 3,990               |
| 2019 | 1,111                   | 268                    | 4,130               |
| 2020 | 1,088                   | 271                    | 4,110               |
| 2021 | 1,186                   | 274                    | 4,350               |

Figure 1 (not drawn to scale here) illustrates the general upward trend in Indonesia’s real GDP between 2017 and 2021, with a slight dip in 2020 due to the global economic slowdown.

**Table 3: Indonesian Tax Rates**

| Type of Tax           | Rate of Tax                                    |
|-----------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Corporate Income Tax  | 22% (planned to be reduced to 20% by 2023)     |
| Personal Income Tax   | Progressive rates up to 35%                    |
| Value-Added Tax (VAT) | 10% (standard rate)                             |

The Indonesian government collects other indirect taxes, including excise taxes on specific goods, but struggles with a relatively narrow tax base. Firms in the manufacturing and services sectors often raise concerns about the efficiency of tax collection.

Recent spending data from the Indonesian Ministry of Finance shows that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in the local economy is approximately 0.8; the government has also increased social transfers to reduce inequality. 

**Table 4: Income Inequality in Indonesia (Selected Data)**

| Year | Gini Coefficient | Poverty Rate (%) |
|------|------------------|-------------------|
| 2017 | 0.39            | 10.6              |
| 2018 | 0.38            | 9.8               |
| 2019 | 0.38            | 9.4               |
| 2020 | 0.39            | 9.8               |
| 2021 | 0.38            | 9.4               |

Analysts worry that despite progress, pockets of poverty remain especially in remote areas. Many policymakers argue that reducing reliance on primary sector exports and improving human capital are essential to achieving Indonesia’s long-term development goals.

parts.content: Explain why a strong reliance on primary sector exports, such as palm oil, might limit Indonesia’s long-term economic development.

- Excessive dependence on these exports can slow diversification into higher value-added industries that generate more robust economic growth.  
- Commodity dependence may discourage investment in human capital and technology, limiting productivity improvements.  
- Fluctuating export revenues can reduce the ability to finance infrastructure, health, and education, hindering sustainable long-term development.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and knowledge of economics, recommend a policy which the Indonesian government could implement to achieve more sustainable and equitable growth. Support the answer with relevant theory and evidence.
parts.markscheme:
- A recommendation is an expansion of government investment in education and skills development to shift reliance away from primary exports and promote higher-value manufacturing and services.  
- This policy could be financed partly through strengthening progressive income taxes (Table 3 shows up to 35%) and improved tax collection, increasing revenues without causing extreme inequalities in disposable income.  
- Higher government spending on human capital would shift the long-run aggregate supply curve to the right (LRAS1 to LRAS2), enabling more sustainable growth by improving productivity and competitiveness (aligning with concerns about diversifying exports beyond palm oil).  



- As incomes rise, consumption increases, stimulated further by the high marginal propensity to consume of 0.8, which magnifies the impact of government spending through the multiplier effect.  
- By improving educational opportunities, income distribution becomes more equitable, reducing the Gini coefficient and tackling the documented poverty pockets in remote areas.  
- Government investment in education raises productivity by improving human capital, shifting LRAS from LRAS1 to LRAS2 This increases potential output from Y1 to Y2, supporting long-term, non-inflationary growth. The diagram reflects how skills development reduces reliance on primary exports, enhances competitiveness, and promotes more equitable and sustainable economic progress.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ab34a8e4-f893-42dd-b0e6-83318cf41038)

- The policy aligns with the aim of limiting reliance on volatile primary export earnings and generating more stable economic growth, supported by domestic demand and diversification, reducing vulnerability to commodity price shocks.  
- Potential drawbacks include the need for efficient tax administration, possible short-term fiscal strain, and the importance of addressing corruption or inefficiencies.  
- However, the long-term benefits of higher productivity, improved living standards, and reduced inequality can outweigh the short-term costs, ensuring more sustainable and equitable growth.

questions.specification:
## Economic Conditions and Policy Directions in Afghanistan

Afghanistan remains one of the world’s least developed countries, facing persistent challenges that include elevated levels of poverty, limited infrastructure, and political instability. Despite these difficulties, the government has undertaken multiple reform programs aimed at stabilizing macroeconomic conditions, diversifying exports, and improving social indicators. Currently, about 47% of the population lives below the national poverty line, although official figures vary by region and season due to the agrarian nature of the economy. Remittances from Afghans abroad constitute a vital source of income for many households, partially offsetting low domestic wages.

Over the past few years, Afghanistan’s real GDP growth has fluctuated between 2.5% and 3.2% per annum. Much of this growth is attributed to agriculture, which accounts for approximately 27% of GDP and employs the majority of the workforce. However, frequent droughts and limited irrigation systems constrain agricultural output, underscoring the importance of improving domestic infrastructure. Meanwhile, inflation has generally remained in the single digits, hovering around 7% in 2021, before stepping up to nearly 9% in 2022. Economists point to higher global food and energy prices as major factors contributing to rising consumer costs.

Domestic industries such as small-scale manufacturing and agricultural processing struggle to secure financing, given the underdeveloped nature of the banking sector. High interest rates and limited access to formal credit often push entrepreneurs to rely on informal loans. Foreign direct investment (FDI) inflows declined from US240 million in 2019 to US$190 million in 2021, partly due to prolonged uncertainty over the country’s security situation. Efforts to attract new investors have included reduced corporate tax rates for selected sectors, especially agribusiness, textile production, and education services.

Trade remains an essential component of Afghanistan’s growth prospects, with key export goods including fruits, nuts, carpets, and medicinal plants. Over half of Afghanistan’s exports currently go to neighboring countries such as Pakistan and India, reflecting the importance of regional trade routes. However, administrative barriers such as complex customs procedures continue to dampen export competitiveness. Additionally, the country’s landlocked position increases transportation costs, limiting the potential for expansion into global markets. Many economists suggest that lowering trade barriers and improving transport infrastructure could stimulate new opportunities for SMEs (small and medium-sized enterprises).

Social indicators reveal a complex development picture. According to the latest estimates, the adult literacy rate is around 43%. Rural areas, in particular, show lower levels of school enrollment, especially for female students. Efforts to improve human capital include government-led initiatives to expand access to primary and secondary education, as well as targeted nutrition programs aimed at reducing child malnutrition. Nonetheless, resource constraints and security issues hamper progress in many provinces.

In the broader push for stability, the government has allocated additional funding to reconstruct rural roads and support the agriculture sector with modern technology. Some policymakers argue that these measures will help reduce unemployment, which official data place around 23%. Critics, however, note the risk of creating fiscal imbalances if public spending continues to outpace government revenues. International donors and organizations remain central to supporting Afghanistan’s development objectives, often linking aid disbursements to specific governance and reform milestones.

A key policy debate revolves around addressing entrenched poverty. Economists stress the need for a well-designed approach to break the poverty cycle, including improved access to health care, educational opportunities, and microfinance services. Together, these measures aim to raise productivity and incomes over time, but persistent conflict and limited institutional capacity remain formidable obstacles.

Below are two tables providing selected economic and social data relevant to Afghanistan’s current situation.

**Table 1: Afghanistan’s Selected Macroeconomic Indicators (2019–2022)**

| Indicator                         | 2019   | 2020   | 2021   | 2022   |
|-----------------------------------|--------|--------|--------|--------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)         | 20.1   | 19.8   | 20.3   | 20.8   |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)          | 2.8    | 2.5    | 3.2    | 2.9    |
| Inflation Rate (%)                | 5.3    | 6.0    | 7.0    | 8.9    |
| FDI Inflows (US$ million)         | 240    | 210    | 190    | 190    |
| Exchange Rate (AFN per US$)       | 75     | 78     | 83     | 88     |

**Table 2: Poverty and Social Indicators for Afghanistan**

| Indicator                                          | 2019  | 2020  | 2021  | 2022  |
|----------------------------------------------------|-------|-------|-------|-------|
| Population (million)                               | 37.6  | 38.4  | 39.3  | 40.1  |
| Poverty Rate (% of population below national line) | 52    | 51    | 49    | 47    |
| Adult Literacy Rate (%)                            | 39    | 41    | 42    | 43    |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                              | 22    | 23    | 23    | 23    |
| Remittance Inflows (US$ million)                   | 490   | 515   | 540   | 560   |

parts.content: Using information from Table 1, calculate the percentage change in Afghanistan’s nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
1. Correct approach:

Percentage change in Afghanistan’s nominal GDP from 2019 to 2022:

$\text{Percentage Change Formula}=\frac{Q_{final}-Q_{initial}}{Q_{initial}}$

- Initial nominal GDP (2019) = US$20.1 billion  
- Final nominal GDP (2022) = US$20.8 billion  
- Change in GDP = 20.8 – 20.1 = US$0.7 billion  

<marks>1 mark</marks>

2. Correct result:

- Percentage change = (0.7 ÷ 20.1) × 100 ≈ 3.48%  

<marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand and supply of currency diagram, explain how an increase in remittance inflows (paragraph 1, Table 2) could affect the exchange rate of the Afghan afghani (AFN).
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/b19eea25-ef6b-42ba-9478-29264cd651ec)

- Higher remittances increase the supply of foreign currency (e.g., US$) in exchange for the local currency AFN.  
- This raises the demand for AFN as recipients convert remittances into AFN for local use.  
- On a currency market diagram, the demand curve for AFN shifts rightward (D1 to D2), causing an appreciation of the AFN if other factors remain constant.  
- The stronger AFN (e1 to e2) might make imports cheaper but exports relatively more expensive due to currency appreciaton.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks>

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a production possibilities curve (PPC) diagram, explain how improvements in infrastructure (paragraph 5) could impact Afghanistan’s long-term productive capacity.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7c17ac60-ca4a-4078-9652-afaddf1d102c)

- Better infrastructure, such as upgraded roads and irrigation, enhances efficiency and reduces costs of production.  
- On a PPC diagram, the entire curve shifts outward (PPC1 to PPC2) as the economy can produce more of both agricultural and manufactured goods.  
- Over time, improved infrastructure raises the maximum potential output, reflecting long-term growth.  
- This can also reduce structural bottlenecks in the economy and encourage investment by improving market efficiency.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a demand and supply diagram, explain how reductions in corporate tax rates for agribusiness (paragraph 3) might affect the equilibrium price and quantity in the agricultural sector.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7a7176a0-42d6-4b82-b00f-2dcb28ac9deb)

- Lower corporate taxes for agribusiness reduce production costs, shifting the supply curve to the right by the amount of tax (S1 -> S2).  
- The new equilibrium in the agricultural sector likely features a lower price (P1) and a higher quantity(Q2) due to supply shift.   
- The resulting increase in output may help farmers expand operations and potentially increase export competitiveness through economies of scale.  
- Consumers benefit from potentially lower prices, and producers may face improved profit margins despite the lower price due to the tax reduction.


<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using a poverty cycle diagram, explain why limited access to education and healthcare (paragraph 7) might trap certain segments of Afghanistan’s population in poverty.
parts.markscheme:

- When households lack quality education and health care, their human capital remains low, limiting productive capacity and income.  
- Low incomes reduce the ability to invest in education or health care (limited savings), perpetuating the cycle.  
- In a poverty cycle diagram, insufficient resources lead to low productivity, which results in limited earnings, preventing any significant improvements in human capital.  
- Over time, the cycle traps certain segments of the population in persistent poverty because of low human capital.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using information from the text/data and your knowledge of economics, evaluate the extent to which reforms targeting FDI attraction, infrastructure development, and poverty reduction can achieve sustainable economic growth in Afghanistan.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 

**Definitions:**

- **Sustainable economic growth**: growth that meets present economic needs without compromising future generations’ ability to meet their own needs, often implying stable macroeconomic conditions, inclusive development, and environmental considerations.  
- **FDI**: foreign direct investment, where foreign companies establish or acquire assets in a country with the intent to manage and operate them.  
- Infrastructure development: improvements in transport, power, water, and communications systems that lower production and transaction costs.  
- **Poverty reduction**: involves policies that lower the number of people living in poverty by improving income and access to basic needs.

**Range of Arguments:**  

- FDI Attraction and Growth  
  - FDI can inject new capital and create jobs, fostering technology transfers that boost productivity. 
  - Increase in FDI will increase AD, causing a rightwards shift from AD1 to AD2, further increasing GDP from Y to Yp and price level from PL1 to PL2 
  - According to Table 1, FDI inflows have declined (US 240 million in 2019 to US$190 million in 2021), partly due to security concerns, limiting potential gains.  
  - Even with reduced corporate tax rates, conflict and political risk may deter many investors, restricting the effectiveness of this policy.  
  - If security improves and investor confidence rises, stable FDI inflows could quicken diversification away from heavy reliance on agriculture.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3df6671c-b980-45b7-84ef-93d3b5e3cff8)

- Infrastructure Development  
  - Upgraded roads and irrigation systems can reduce costs for farmers and manufacturers, shifting the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to the right.  
  - Table 1 suggests modest growth rates (2.5–3.2%); better infrastructure could raise these figures over the long term by improving productivity.  
  - Infrastructure plans may strain government budgets, risking fiscal imbalances if revenue is insufficient, especially when donor support is conditional.  
  - If managed responsibly, improved infrastructure can be a catalyst for sustainable growth by enhancing trade competitiveness and encouraging private investment.

- Poverty Reduction Strategies  
  - Addressing poverty (47% in 2022, Table 2) through expanded education, healthcare, and microfinance can increase human capital and productivity.  
  - Higher literacy rates (now 43%) improve labor market outcomes and encourage entrepreneurship, raising long-term potential output.  
  - Security concerns, limited institutional capacity, and uneven resource distribution hamper the success of poverty-reduction programs.  
  - If effectively implemented, these measures can break the poverty cycle, fueling inclusive and more sustainable growth.


**Evaluation and Conclusion**  

- Policies to attract FDI may partially succeed if security improves and the business environment becomes more predictable. However, gains are vulnerable to persistent conflict and regulatory uncertainties.  
- Infrastructure development can reduce structural bottlenecks and expand productive capacity, but requires careful fiscal management to avoid unsustainable debt, which can harm economic stability and growth prospects. 
- Poverty reduction measures support long-term growth by building human capital, although actual implementation depends on institutional constraints and security as stability and governance affect effectiveness.  
- The reforms could significantly promote sustainable growth if addressed holistically ensuring macroeconomic stability, adequate governance, and continued donor engagement. The overarching barriers of political instability and limited capacity remain critical obstacles that may reduce the effectiveness of even well-designed policies.

questions.specification:
Thailand is an upper-middle-income country located in Southeast Asia, with a population of approximately 70 million people. Tourism, manufacturing, and agriculture are the main drivers of Thailand’s economy. Before the global pandemic, tourism contributed close to 20% of Thailand’s gross domestic product (GDP). Major exports include electronics, automobiles, and agricultural products (such as rice and rubber). Unemployment rates in Thailand are traditionally very low compared to global averages, but the uneven distribution of income remains a concern.

Despite regular efforts to diversify, Thailand still faces challenges related to income inequality, fluctuating global demand for exports, and changes in tourist arrivals. In 2022, the impacts of changing global economic conditions and rising fuel prices affected the costs of production and transportation in Thailand’s key export sectors, including rice and rubber.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators of Thailand (2019–2022)**

| Year | Real GDP (US$ billions) | Annual Real GDP Growth (%) | Inflation Rate (%) | Unemployment Rate (%) | Gini Coefficient |
|------|--------------------------|-----------------------------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------|
| 2019 | 502                      | 2.3                         | 1.0                 | 1.0                    | 0.42             |
| 2020 | 501                      | -6.1                        | 0.8                 | 2.0                    | 0.43             |
| 2021 | 505                      | 1.6                         | 1.2                 | 1.9                    | 0.43             |
| 2022 | 544                      | 2.8                         | 6.1                 | 1.5                    | 0.43             |


In 2022, the government introduced an infrastructural investment package worth 100 billion baht (THB) in an attempt to boost aggregate demand (AD). Economists estimate that the marginal propensity to consume (MPC) in Thailand is approximately 0.75.

**Table 2: Market for Thai Rubber Exports (2022 Estimates)**

| Price per Metric Ton (US$) | Quantity Demanded (million metric tons per year) | Quantity Supplied on Domestic Market (million metric tons per year) |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 1,800 | 3.5 | 2.8 |
| 2,000 | 3.0 | 3.0 |

Economists estimate that the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Thai rubber exports increases in magnitude when prices rise. The Thai Rubber Association reported that a 10% increase in the price of rubber from US\$1,800 to US$1,980 per metric ton led to a 7% reduction in exports sold to foreign buyers.

**Table 3: Taxation in Thailand (2022)**

| Type of Tax                 | Tax Rate                                   |
| --------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ |
| Corporate Income Tax (CIT)  | 20%                                        |
| Personal Income Tax (PIT)   | Progressive rates from 5% to 35%          |
| Value Added Tax (VAT)       | Standard rate of 7% on eligible products  |

parts.content: Using the information from Table 2, calculate the price elasticity of demand (PED) for Thai rubber exports when the price increases from USD1,800 to USD1,980 per metric ton. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- % change in price = (1,980 – 1,800) ÷ 1,800 × 100% = 10%  
- % change in quantity demanded = –7% (given)  
- PED = (% change in quantity demanded) ÷ (% change in price) = (–7%) ÷ (10%) = –0.7

parts.content: In 2022, real GDP increased from USD505 billion to USD544 billion. Calculate the approximate percentage change in real GDP growth for 2022 using these figures. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
- Change in GDP = 544 – 505 = 39  
- % change in GDP = (39 ÷ 505) × 100% ≈ 7.7%

parts.content: Refer to Table 3. Suppose a domestic pharmaceutical company in Thailand has pre-tax profits of 10 million THB and pays corporate income tax (CIT) at the rate shown. Using the information in Table 3, calculate the amount of CIT paid by this company.

Assume a small IT firm in Bangkok makes a taxable profit of 400,000 THB, subject to personal income tax (PIT) on the owner’s income. If the average PIT rate for this level of income is 15%, calculate how much personal income tax the owner pays.
Define the term “marginal propensity to consume.”
parts.markscheme: The marginal propensity to consume is the fraction of any additional income that is spent on consumption rather than saved.

parts.content: Using an aggregate demand and aggregate supply (AD/AS) diagram, explain how the government’s 100 billion baht infrastructural investment package (mentioned in the text) could affect real GDP and the price level in Thailand in the short run.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/03a62e1a-8fdc-4e95-8cb0-4a30d72c7702)

- An increase in government spending shifts the AD curve to the right (AD1 -> AD2), indicating a rise in overall demand in the economy.  
- In the short run, this raises real GDP (Y -> Yp) and increases the price level (pl1 -> pl2), due to stronger demand pressures.  
- The shift is from the initial equilibrium, where AD intersects AS, to a new equilibrium with higher output (Yp) and a higher average price level (pl2).  

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using the data in Table 2, calculate the excess demand or excess supply in the Thai rubber market at the price of US\$1,800 per metric ton
parts.markscheme:
- Step 1: Identify relevant figures
  At US\$1,800 per metric ton:

- Quantity demanded = 3.5 million metric tons

- Quantity supplied = 2.8 million metric tons

- Step 2: Calculate excess demand

$$
3.5 - 2.8 = 0.7 \text{ million metric tons}
$$

- Step 3: State the outcome

 Answer: Excess demand (shortage) of 0.7 million metric tons

parts.content: Using the information in Table 1 and the text above, explain two ways in which income inequality might hinder Thailand’s long-term economic growth.
parts.markscheme:
- Lower-income households have limited spending power, which reduces consumption levels in the economy and may limit aggregate demand and investment over time.  
- Uneven distribution of income discourages the accumulation of human capital, as households with lower incomes may struggle to invest in education or skills, constraining productivity and potential growth.

parts.content: Using the text/data provided and your knowledge of economics, recommend a policy that the Thai government could implement to reduce income inequality while sustaining economic growth. Justify your recommendation.
parts.markscheme:
- Income inequality can be defined as an uneven distribution of income across the population, measured in Thailand by a Gini coefficient of 0.43. Economic growth refers to the increase in a country’s real output over time, as reflected by rising real GDP.  
- A viable policy could be the expansion of government spending on education and vocational training, combined with a progressive tax structure, to enhance human capital and address unequal income distribution.  
- According to the text, persistent income inequality might suppress aggregate demand and long-term growth. Additional investment in training and education, funded by progressive personal income tax rates, can bolster productivity and increase employability, especially where the text shows concerns about uneven access to opportunity.  
- Raising human capital boosts Thailand’s manufacturing and export competitiveness in electronics, automobiles, and agricultural products, maintaining growth while narrowing skill gaps that contribute to inequality.  
- The policy aligns with existing progressive tax rates (ranging from 5% to 35%), which can be deployed without undermining production incentives, potentially enhancing fiscal revenue for targeted public spending.  
- Greater public investment in human capital and infrastructure benefits lower-income groups, fostering a more equitable distribution of income and supporting aggregate demand through higher consumption.  
- In the long run, a better-educated labor force supports higher rates of innovation and productivity, leading to sustainable growth beyond short-term gains.  
-Government spending on education improves human capital, causing the long-run aggregate supply (LRAS) curve to shift right from LRAS1 to LRAS2. This leads to an increase in real output from Y1 to Y2, showing sustainable, long-term economic growth. The diagram illustrates how better education boosts productivity, reduces inequality, and supports Thailand’s long-term development goals.

![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/3d094820-8561-497c-9fa8-fac01c5de9e8)

- The policy can be justified by reference to the increase in real GDP from US\$505 billion to US$544 billion, suggesting capacity for spending on developmental programs; investing in education ensures that growth does not perpetuate existing inequalities.  
- Potential drawbacks include the need for careful budget allocation to avoid excessive government debt and to manage opportunity costs; however, strengthening human capital can offset these concerns by raising productivity growth.  
- In conclusion, a policy focused on expanding education and skills development, financed by a fair and progressive tax regime, reduces inequality while fostering a sustained rise in real GDP over time.

parts.content: Explain the difficulties that exist in measuring unemployment.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions
- **Unemployment Rate**: The number of unemployed people expressed as a percentage of the labor force.
- **Labor Force**: The total number of people who are employed and unemployed but actively seeking work.
- **Discouraged Workers**: Individuals who have stopped looking for work because they believe no jobs are available for them.

### Explanation
- **Understanding Unemployment Measurement**:
  - The unemployment rate is a key indicator of economic health, but it has limitations in accurately reflecting the true state of unemployment.
  
- **Challenges in Measuring Unemployment**:
  - **Exclusion of Discouraged Workers**:
    - Discouraged workers are not counted in the unemployment rate because they are not actively seeking employment.
    - This exclusion can lead to an underestimation of the true unemployment rate.
  
  - **Underemployment**:
    - Individuals working part-time or in jobs that do not utilize their skills are considered employed, even if they seek full-time work.
    - This can mask the extent of labor underutilization in the economy.
  
  - **Informal Economy**:
    - Workers in the informal sector may not be captured in official statistics, leading to inaccuracies.
    - This is particularly relevant in developing countries where informal employment is prevalent.
  
  - **Survey Limitations**:
    - Unemployment data is often collected through surveys, which can be subject to sampling errors and biases.
    - Respondents may misreport their employment status, either intentionally or unintentionally.
  
### Diagram
- **Labor Market Diagram**: While a diagram is not strictly necessary for this question, a simple labor market diagram can be used to illustrate the concept of unemployment. It should indicate the equilibrium where labor supply meets labor demand, and the gap representing unemployment.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/a590f609-c402-4c0f-a0d2-b45338d719dd)

parts.content: Using real-world examples, evaluate transfer payments as a measure to reduce poverty.
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include:

### Definitions
1. **Transfer Payments**: Transfer payments are redistributions of income by the government to individuals or households without any exchange of goods or services.
2. **Poverty**: The inability to meet standard consumption needs. These standards can be absolute or relative.
3. **Redistribution of Income**: A policy intended to adjust the distribution of wealth by transferring income from wealthier to poorer individuals.

### Economic Theory
- **Purpose of Transfer Payments**:
  - Transfer payments aim to provide financial support to low-income individuals, thereby increasing their disposable income.
  - By increasing disposable income, transfer payments can help individuals meet basic needs, reducing absolute poverty.
  
- **Mechanism**:
  - Transfer payments are funded through taxation, primarily from higher-income groups, and redistributed to lower-income groups.
  - This redistribution can lead to a more equitable income distribution, potentially reducing income inequality.

- **Impact on Aggregate Demand**:
  - Increased disposable income for low-income individuals can lead to higher consumption, as these individuals have a higher marginal propensity to consume.
  - This increase in consumption can boost aggregate demand, potentially leading to economic growth and job creation.

### Diagram:
  - **Lorenz Curve**: Illustrates income distribution. Transfer payments can shift the Lorenz Curve closer to the line of equality, indicating a more equitable distribution of income.
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/510a1fb9-5f5e-4d46-9b44-a2833ed6108f)


### Evaluation
- **Stakeholders**:
  - Beneficiaries: Low-income individuals benefit directly from increased financial support.
  - Taxpayers: Higher-income individuals may face higher taxes to fund transfer payments, potentially reducing their disposable income.
  - Government: Must balance fiscal sustainability with social welfare objectives.

- **Long-run vs. Short-run**:
  - Short-run: Immediate reduction in poverty levels as individuals receive financial support.
  - Long-run: Potential dependency on government support if not coupled with policies promoting self-sufficiency, such as education and job training.

- **Advantages vs. Disadvantages**:
  - Advantages: Directly reduces poverty, improves living standards, and can stimulate economic activity through increased consumption.
  - Disadvantages: May lead to fiscal strain if not managed properly, potential disincentive to work if benefits are too generous, and possible inefficiencies in targeting the truly needy.

- **Prioritize**:
  - Effective targeting and efficient administration are crucial to ensure that transfer payments reach those in need without creating significant fiscal burdens.
  - Complementary policies, such as education and job training, are essential to ensure long-term poverty reduction and economic self-sufficiency.

- **Real-World Example**:
    - Provides food-purchasing assistance to low-income individuals.
    - In 2020, SNAP helped lift approximately 3.2 million people out of poverty.
    - However, concerns about dependency and fiscal sustainability persist.

### Conclusion
- Transfer payments can effectively reduce poverty in the short term by increasing disposable income for low-income individuals.
- Long-term success requires complementary policies that promote economic self-sufficiency.
- Effective targeting and fiscal management are essential to maximize benefits and minimize drawbacks.

questions.specification:
## Sweden’s Economic Landscape

Sweden, situated in Northern Europe, has long been recognized for its robust welfare system and stable macroeconomic fundamentals. In 2022, Sweden recorded a real GDP growth of 2.8%, a slight moderation compared to the 3.1% observed in 2021. Meanwhile, the headline inflation rate rose from 2.6% to 3.9% over the same period, placing additional pressure on household incomes and consumer spending. The Swedish government has maintained a comparatively low public debt ratio of around 37% of GDP in 2022, reflecting a tradition of prudent fiscal management. However, some economists argue that Sweden’s aging population could eventually strain this fiscal position, notably through rising health-care expenditures.

In the labor market, unemployment remains relatively low by European standards, standing at 6.0% in early 2023. Yet certain sectors, such as advanced manufacturing, face shortages of skilled labor, prompting policymakers to focus on education and vocational training programs. Various industries particularly robotics, pharmaceuticals, and green technology have benefitted from government incentives aimed at promoting research and development. Each year, approximately 1.1 billion Swedish kronor is allocated to specialized grants in sustainable energy innovations.

On the microeconomic front, Sweden is committed to reducing negative externalities by implementing a high carbon tax of approximately US$130 per metric ton of CO₂. This measure incentivizes firms to shift toward cleaner technologies, thereby lowering emissions. Some small and medium-sized enterprises, however, argue that the carbon tax raises production costs compared to international competitors, particularly those in countries with less stringent environmental policies.

Sweden’s membership in the European Union (EU) means that its trade policy is closely tied to the EU’s common external tariffs. Exports account for about 45% of Sweden’s GDP, with key foreign markets including Germany, the United Kingdom, and the United States. Recently, a partnership with South Korea has boosted Sweden’s technological exports, including electronics and precision machinery. In 2022, Sweden’s trade surplus stood at US$8.5 billion. However, the global uncertainties have led to more volatile exchange rate movements for the Swedish krona (SEK), prompting the central bank (Riksbank) to occasionally adjust interest rates to stabilize capital flows.

Domestic consumption patterns also continue to evolve. An increasing proportion of the population buys goods and services through digital platforms, reflecting Sweden’s high internet penetration rate. In 2022, e-commerce accounted for over 18% of total retail sales, compared to just 10% a few years prior. Additionally, Sweden has a progressive tax system, which aims to balance income distribution and provide universal social benefits, including health-care, education, and a comprehensive pension scheme. Notably, the Gini coefficient has hovered around 0.28–0.30 over the past decade, illustrating relatively low income inequality compared to global averages.

Other recent developments include Sweden’s continued efforts to cultivate foreign direct investment (FDI). Despite global economic uncertainties, net FDI inflows reached US$12 billion in 2021, particularly in the renewable energy and biotechnology sectors. At the same time, Swedish policy makers remain cautious about excessive reliance on export and investment inflows, advocating for a diversified approach to sustained economic growth. Technological innovation, labor market adaptability, and environmental stewardship remain at the core of Sweden’s long-term development strategy.

**Table 1: Selected Macroeconomic Indicators for Sweden**  

| Indicator                                 | 2019     | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     |
|-------------------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Nominal GDP (US$ billion)                | 560      | 540      | 580      | 590      |
| Real GDP Growth Rate (%)                 | 2.0      | -2.8     | 3.1      | 2.8      |
| Inflation Rate (%)                       | 1.5      | 0.7      | 2.6      | 3.9      |
| Unemployment Rate (%)                    | 6.3      | 8.2      | 6.5      | 6.0      |
| Public Debt (% of GDP)                   | 35       | 39       | 38       | 37       |
| Interest Rate (Policy Rate, %)           | 0.25     | 0.00     | 0.75     | 1.50     |

**Table 2: Sweden’s International Trade Data**

| Trade Indicator         | 2019     | 2020     | 2021     | 2022     |
|-------------------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|
| Exports (US$ billion)   | 250      | 230      | 265      | 280      |
| Imports (US$ billion)   | 240      | 225      | 260      | 271      |
| Trade Balance (US$ bn)  | 10       | 5        | 5        | 9        |
| Top Export Partner (%)  | Germany (13%)| Germany (13%)| Germany (12%)| Germany (12%)|
| Top Import Partner (%)  | Norway (10%) | Norway (11%) | Norway (10%) | Norway (10%) |

parts.content: Using a negative externalities diagram, explain how Sweden’s carbon tax could reduce the production of goods and services that create harmful emissions (Paragraph 3).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/2a80a2a3-3d34-46c3-94b3-de520d0b849b)

- Sweden’s carbon tax aims to internalize the external cost of pollution by shifting the supply curve (MPC) upward toward the MSC curve. 
- As production costs rise, market output contracts from the free-market equilibrium toward the socially optimal equilibrium, lowering emissions.
-  The higher price encourages firms to adopt cleaner technologies, reducing the welfare loss associated with these negative externalities.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>

parts.content: Using an exchange rate diagram, explain how changes in Sweden’s interest rate might affect the value of the Swedish krona (SEK) against other currencies (Paragraph 4).
parts.markscheme:
Answers may include 
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/7a11381a-7f3c-4983-87d1-3d3c804d6215)

- An increase in Sweden’s interest rate from 0.00% in 2020 to 1.50% in 2022 raises the return on Swedish financial assets, increasing demand for SEK. On an exchange rate diagram, the demand curve for SEK shifts right, from D1 to D2, causing an appreciation of the currency. 
- Conversely, a rate cut would shift demand left, leading to a depreciation.

<marks> Award 2 marks for correct diagram OR explanation</marks> 

<marks> Award 4 marks for correct diagram AND explanation</marks>
